title: Light It Up
description: Turn on the display and show text on your M5StickC Plus 2.
story_text: Chip blinks his tiny eyes open and looks around. Everything is dark! "Hey, is anybody there?" he whispers. "It's so dark in here... I can't see a thing! Can you help me turn on my screen? I bet we can make it glow!" Help Chip light up his display by writing your very first program!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Hello World!", 10, 50, 0xFFFFFF)

hints.text: First, you need to clear the screen. Look for the Display Clear block in the toolbox!
Now add a Display Text block after the clear block. Type your message where it says "Hello!"
Connect the Clear Display block to the Display Text block. Set x to 10 and y to 50, then type any message you like!

story.title: Wake Up, Chip!
story.narrative: Chip wakes up in the dark and needs help turning on his screen. Your first program!

create.title: My First Message
create.prompt: What message would you display on your M5Stick? Write it and show the world!

explore.title: Turn On the Display
explore.instructions: Drag the display block to turn on the screen. Add a text block to show your message!
explore.device_interaction: Display turns on and shows your text.
explore.simulator_fallback: See your text appear on the virtual M5Stick screen.

celebrate.title: Programmer!
celebrate.summary: You wrote your very first program! The display lit up because of YOUR code.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child wrote their first program, learning that code is instructions a computer follows.

title: Color My World
description: Draw colored shapes on the display using rectangles and circles.
story_text: Chip is bouncing up and down with excitement. "Wow, you turned on the screen! That was amazing! But it's a bit boring with just words, don't you think? I LOVE colors! Red, blue, green -- all of them! Can you help me paint some shapes on my tiny screen? Let's make it look like a rainbow party!" Time to become a digital artist!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.fillRect(10, 10, 50, 50, 0xFF0000)
M5.Lcd.fillRect(70, 10, 50, 50, 0x0000FF)
M5.Lcd.fillCircle(67, 100, 25, 0x00FF00)

hints.text: Start by clearing the screen with a dark background. Then look in the Display category for shape blocks!
Drag out a Draw Rectangle block. You need to set x, y, width, and height numbers. Try x=10, y=10, width=50, height=50!
Add a rectangle with x=10, y=10, width=50, height=50 in red. Then add another rectangle and a circle with different colors!

story.title: Rainbow Party!
story.narrative: Chip wants to paint his screen with every color of the rainbow. Help him with code blocks!

create.title: My Digital Painting
create.prompt: Use shapes and colors to draw a picture. A sunset? A robot? Let your creativity flow!

explore.title: Draw with Code
explore.instructions: Use rectangle and circle blocks to draw colored shapes on the display.
explore.device_interaction: Display shows your colored shapes.
explore.simulator_fallback: See shapes appear on the virtual screen.

celebrate.title: Digital Artist!
celebrate.summary: You drew shapes with code! Every app and game you use draws graphics just like this.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child learned to use code to draw shapes and set colors, understanding that graphics are code-driven.

title: Button Magic
description: React to button presses to make things happen on screen.
story_text: Chip is poking at the two little buttons on the M5Stick. "Hey, what do these clicky things do? I pressed one and nothing happened! That's no fun at all. I bet YOU can make them do something awesome. What if pressing Button A made the screen turn red, and Button B made it turn blue? That would be SO COOL!" Let's wire up the buttons to make Chip happy!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Press a button!", 10, 50, 0xFFFFFF)

while True:
    M5.update()
    if M5.BtnA.isPressed():
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0xFF0000)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("Button A!", 20, 100, 0xFFFFFF)
    if M5.BtnB.isPressed():
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x0000FF)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("Button B!", 20, 100, 0xFFFFFF)
    time.sleep(0.1)

hints.text: You need a Forever loop to keep checking the buttons. Look in the Loops category for one!
Inside the loop, add an If block from Logic. Then put a "Button A pressed?" block from the Buttons category as the condition.
Put a Clear Display (red) and Display Text block inside the If for Button A. Then add another If block for Button B with blue!

story.title: Magic Buttons!
story.narrative: Chip pokes the buttons and nothing happens. Help him make the buttons DO something!

create.title: My Button App
create.prompt: Design an app with two buttons. What does each one do? Make it fun!

explore.title: Wire the Buttons
explore.instructions: Use if-button-pressed blocks to make things happen when buttons are pressed.
explore.device_interaction: Press real buttons to trigger screen changes.
explore.simulator_fallback: Click virtual buttons to trigger your code.

celebrate.title: Button Master!
celebrate.summary: You made buttons interactive! This is how every app and game handles user input.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child learned about conditionals (if-then) through button event handling.

title: Beep Boop
description: Make the buzzer play tones and musical notes.
story_text: Chip is humming to himself. Well, trying to -- but no sound comes out! "I have a tiny speaker inside me, but I don't know how to use it! Can you help me make some sounds? I want to play music! Maybe we could play a little song -- like do, re, mi! Different numbers make different notes. Big numbers are high and squeaky, small numbers are low and rumbly. Let's make some noise!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_buzzer_tone" x="20" y="20"><value name="FREQUENCY"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">262</field></shadow></value><value name="DURATION"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">500</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
# Play "do re mi" - three notes!
M5.Speaker.tone(262, 500)
time.sleep(0.6)
M5.Speaker.tone(294, 500)
time.sleep(0.6)
M5.Speaker.tone(330, 500)
time.sleep(0.6)
M5.Speaker.tone(349, 500)
time.sleep(0.6)
M5.Speaker.tone(392, 800)
time.sleep(1.0)
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Music time!", 10, 50, 0xFFFF00)

hints.text: Open the Sound category and drag out a Play Tone block. The first number is how high or low the sound is!
To play more than one note, add a Wait block between each Play Tone block so the notes don't all happen at once. Try 0.6 seconds!
Use these numbers for a musical scale: 262 (do), 294 (re), 330 (mi), 349 (fa), 392 (so). Put a Wait block after each tone!

story.title: Music Maker!
story.narrative: Chip discovers his buzzer can play sounds. Help him make music with code!

create.title: My Ringtone
create.prompt: Program a short ringtone or alarm sound. Something catchy that people will remember!

explore.title: Program the Buzzer
explore.instructions: Use tone blocks to play sounds. Chain them together to make a melody!
explore.device_interaction: Buzzer plays your programmed tones.
explore.simulator_fallback: Hear your tones through browser audio.

celebrate.title: Sound Coder!
celebrate.summary: You programmed music with code! Loops make melodies repeat automatically.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child learned about loops and sequences by programming musical tones.

title: LED Light Show
description: Control the built-in LED to create a colorful light show.
story_text: Chip gasps. "Did you know I have a secret light hidden inside me? It's called an LED and it can glow in different colors! Red... green... blue... it's like a tiny traffic light! But right now it's turned off and I feel kind of dim. Can you make it blink and change colors? Let's put on a LIGHT SHOW! We can make it flash red, then green, then blue, over and over. Disco time!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_led_set" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">red</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Light show!", 10, 50, 0xFFFFFF)
for i in range(3):
    Power.led(255, 0, 0)
    M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0xFF0000)
    time.sleep(0.5)
    Power.led(0, 255, 0)
    M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x00FF00)
    time.sleep(0.5)
    Power.led(0, 0, 255)
    M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x0000FF)
    time.sleep(0.5)
Power.led(0, 0, 0)
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Show over!", 15, 50, 0xFFFFFF)

hints.text: Find the LED category and drag out a Set LED block. Pick a color and see what happens! Then add a Wait block after it.
To make it blink, set the LED to a color, wait, then set it to a different color. Try red, wait, green, wait, blue, wait! You can use a "repeat" loop to do it more than once.
Use a Repeat loop set to 3 times. Inside, put: Set LED red, Wait 0.5 sec, Set LED green, Wait 0.5 sec, Set LED blue, Wait 0.5 sec. End with Set LED off!

story.title: Light Show Time!
story.narrative: Chip wants to put on a dazzling LED light show. Help him program light patterns!

create.title: My Light Show
create.prompt: Design a light show that goes with a mood or song. Blinking, fading, and pulsing!

explore.title: Program the LED
explore.instructions: Use LED blocks with loops and delays to create light patterns and shows.
explore.device_interaction: LED pulses and blinks according to your code.
explore.simulator_fallback: Virtual LED shows your programmed patterns.

celebrate.title: Light Show Director!
celebrate.summary: You created a light show with code! Loops and timing make lights dance.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child combined loops, timing, and LED control to create sequential light patterns.

title: Code Vocabulary!
description: Learn the words programmers use! Variable, loop, function, bug -- see each coding word in action on your M5Stick with examples!
story_text: Before we start coding, let's learn some important words that coders use! Flip each card to discover what it means.
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="m5_display_text"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">Code Words!</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">yellow</field></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Code Words!", 15, 10)
words = [("LOOP", "repeats things"), ("BUG", "a mistake"), ("VARIABLE", "stores data"), ("SEQUENCE", "steps in order")]
y = 35
for word, meaning in words:
    Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(word + ":", 5, y)
    Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(meaning, 5, y + 15)
    Speaker.tone(400, 100)
    time.sleep(1)
    y = y + 35

hints.text: Programmers have special words! Lets learn them: LOOP, BUG, VARIABLE, SEQUENCE. Each one means something important!
Start with a clear screen and title. Display the first word: LOOP -- it means something that REPEATS!
Show each word in green, and its meaning in white below it. Add a beep and wait between each.
BUG = a mistake in code. VARIABLE = stores data (like a box). SEQUENCE = steps in order.
You just built a coding dictionary on your M5Stick! These words will help you understand programming!

back.text: A mistake in code that makes it not work right
Doing something over and over again
A box that holds information
A set of steps you can reuse
Finding and fixing mistakes in code

activities.prompt: Flip each card to learn a coding word!

title: Debug the Bug!
description: Practice debugging! Your M5Stick program has blocks in the wrong order. Rearrange them to fix the bug and make the countdown work correctly!
story_text: Uh oh! Chip wrote some instructions but made mistakes. Can you find the bug in each set of instructions?
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_text" x="20" y="20"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">Liftoff!</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">25</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">red</field><next><block type="m5_display_text"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">3...2...1...</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">40</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">yellow</field></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Countdown!", 20, 10)
for i in range(3, 0, -1):
    Lcd.fillRect(40, 50, 60, 40, 0x000000)
    Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(i) + "...", 50, 60)
    Speaker.tone(400, 200)
    time.sleep(1)
Lcd.fillRect(0, 40, 135, 60, 0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFF0000, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("LIFTOFF!", 25, 60)
Speaker.tone(800, 500)
Power.setLed(255)
time.sleep(1)
Power.setLed(0)

hints.text: DEBUGGING means finding and fixing mistakes! Look at the starter blocks -- "Liftoff!" appears BEFORE the countdown. Thats the bug!
Fix the order: first show the countdown (3, 2, 1), THEN show Liftoff! Move blocks around by dragging them.
Add a clear screen at the very beginning. Then display countdown numbers one at a time with waits.
After the countdown reaches 0, THEN show "LIFTOFF!" in big red text with a sound effect!
Add an LED flash for the rocket launch. The key to debugging: check the ORDER of your steps!

questions.hint: After putting on peanut butter, you need to close the sandwich!
questions.prompt: Chip wants to make a sandwich: 1) Get bread 2) Put on peanut butter 3) Eat sandwich. What is missing?

options.text: Put on the top bread slice

questions.hint: A face needs eyes AND a mouth!

questions.hint: You need to fill the watering can FIRST, then pour!
questions.prompt: Chip wants to water a plant: 1) Pour water in pot 2) Fill watering can. What is wrong?

title: Pixel Art
description: Draw individual pixels to make simple images on the tiny screen.
story_text: Chip squints at the screen. "Did you know this screen is made of teeny tiny dots called pixels? Each one can be any color! It's like having a super tiny piece of graph paper. If you color in the right dots, you can draw anything -- a heart, a star, even a little smiley face! Let's start small and draw a simple picture using just dots. I want to see a heart! Can you make one for me?"

M5.Lcd.drawString("Pixel Heart!", 20, 10, 0xFFFFFF)

M5.Lcd.fillRect(77, 50, p, p, 0xFF0000)
M5.Lcd.fillRect(17, 60, p, p, 0xFF0000)
M5.Lcd.fillRect(27, 60, p, p, 0xFF0000)
M5.Lcd.fillRect(37, 60, p, p, 0xFF0000)
M5.Lcd.fillRect(47, 60, p, p, 0xFF0000)

hints.text: Start with a Clear Display block to make the background black. Then use Draw Rectangle blocks to draw small squares -- those are your pixels!
Make each rectangle small (like 10 wide and 10 tall) and pick a color. Place them next to each other by changing the x and y numbers by 10 each time.
To make a heart: put two red squares at the top for the bumps, then make each row below a little wider, then narrower toward the bottom. Use width=10, height=10 for each pixel!

story.title: Pixel by Pixel
story.narrative: Chip wants to draw detailed pictures on his tiny screen using individual pixels!

create.title: My Pixel Character
create.prompt: Design a pixel art character. Give it a name and personality!

explore.title: Code Pixel Art
explore.instructions: Use pixel blocks to color individual dots on the display grid. Build pictures one pixel at a time!
explore.device_interaction: Display shows your pixel art.
explore.simulator_fallback: See pixels appear on the virtual screen.

celebrate.title: Pixel Artist Coder!
celebrate.summary: You created pixel art with code! Each pixel has an x,y coordinate position.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child learned coordinate-based drawing, connecting coding to math (x,y positioning).

title: Shape Painter
description: Combine rectangles and circles to make pictures.
story_text: Chip claps his hands. "Those pixels were awesome, but you know what? We have SHAPE superpowers too! We can draw big rectangles and circles in one go -- no need to place each pixel! I dare you to draw a picture using just shapes. Maybe a house? Or how about a robot face? Rectangles for the head, circles for the eyes! Let's get creative!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="m5_display_rect"><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">40</field></shadow></value><value name="WIDTH"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">95</field></shadow></value><value name="HEIGHT"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">95</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">yellow</field><field name="FILL">TRUE</field></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.fillRect(20, 40, 95, 95, 0xFFFF00)
M5.Lcd.fillCircle(47, 70, 12, 0x0000FF)
M5.Lcd.fillCircle(87, 70, 12, 0x0000FF)
M5.Lcd.fillCircle(47, 70, 5, 0x000000)
M5.Lcd.fillCircle(87, 70, 5, 0x000000)
M5.Lcd.fillRect(40, 105, 55, 12, 0xFF0000)
M5.Lcd.fillRect(62, 20, 10, 20, 0x00FF00)
M5.Lcd.fillCircle(67, 18, 6, 0xFF0000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("I'm a robot!", 20, 150, 0xFFFFFF)

hints.text: The starter gives you a head shape. Now add circles for eyes! Look for the Draw Circle block in the Display category.
For the eyes, put two circles inside the yellow rectangle. Try x=47, y=70, radius=12 for the left eye, and x=87, y=70 for the right eye!
Add blue circles for eyes (radius 12), tiny black circles inside for pupils (radius 5), a red rectangle for the mouth (x=40, y=105, width=55, height=12), and a green rectangle on top for the antenna!

story.title: Shape Combiner
story.narrative: Chip learns that complex pictures are just simple shapes combined together!

create.title: My Shape Scene
create.prompt: Build a complete scene (house, tree, sun, clouds) using only code shape blocks!

explore.title: Combine Shapes
explore.instructions: Layer rectangles, circles, triangles, and lines to create complex pictures from simple shapes.
explore.device_interaction: Display renders your layered shape composition.

celebrate.title: Shape Painter!
celebrate.summary: You built complex images from simple shapes! That is how all computer graphics work.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child practiced computational decomposition by building complex images from simple geometric primitives.

title: Animation Station
description: Use loops to create simple animations on the screen.
story_text: Chip's eyes go wide. "You know what's even cooler than a picture? A picture that MOVES! That's called animation! Cartoons work by showing lots of pictures really fast. We can do the same thing! Draw something, wait a tiny bit, erase it, draw it somewhere new -- WHOOSH, it's moving! Let's make a ball that bounces across the screen. Ready? Let's bring our screen to life!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Bouncing ball!", 15, 10, 0xFFFFFF)
x = 10
for i in range(25):
    M5.Lcd.fillCircle(x, 120, 12, 0x000000)
    x = x + 5
    M5.Lcd.fillCircle(x, 120, 12, 0x00FF00)
    time.sleep(0.1)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Hooray!", 40, 160, 0xFFFF00)

hints.text: An animation needs a loop! Find the Repeat block in the Loops category. Inside the loop, draw a circle, wait, then clear the screen.
To make the ball move, you need to change where you draw it each time. Instead of erasing the whole screen, draw a black circle where the old ball was, then draw a green circle at the new spot.
Use a Repeat loop set to 25 times. Inside: draw a black circle at the old position to erase it, then draw a green circle 5 pixels to the right. Add a Wait of 0.1 seconds so you can see it move!

story.title: Bring It to Life!
story.narrative: Chip wants his drawings to MOVE. Discover that animation is just drawing + timing + loops!

create.title: My Animation
create.prompt: Create an animation. A bouncing ball? A flying bird? A scrolling message? Code it to life!

explore.title: Animate with Code
explore.instructions: Use loops and timing to move shapes across the screen. Clear and redraw to create smooth animation!
explore.device_interaction: Display shows your animated shapes moving.
explore.simulator_fallback: See animations play on the virtual screen.

celebrate.title: Animator Coder!
celebrate.summary: You made things move with code! Animation is loops + drawing + timing. Games work the same way!
celebrate.parent_note: Your child learned animation fundamentals through code: game loops, screen clearing, and frame-by-frame redrawing.

title: Shake It Up!
description: Detect shakes with the motion sensor and react on screen.
story_text: Chip discovers he can feel movement! "Whoa, something is tingling inside me! I think I have a motion sensor -- it's like a tiny brain that feels when I move! Can you shake me and see what happens? I bet we can make the screen say something when you shake me! Like a magic trick!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Shake me!", 20, 50, 0xFFFFFF)
while True:
    M5.update()
    if shake_detected():
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0xFF00FF)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("SHAKEN!", 25, 60, 0xFFFFFF)
        M5.Speaker.tone(880, 200)
        time.sleep(0.5)
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("Shake me!", 20, 50, 0xFFFFFF)
    time.sleep(0.1)

hints.text: Look in the Sensors category for the Shake Detected block. This checks if the M5Stick has been shaken!
Put the Shake Detected block inside a Forever loop with an If block. When a shake is detected, change the screen color and show a message!
Inside the If block: clear the screen with a fun color, show "SHAKEN!", play a tone (880), and wait 0.5 seconds. Then reset the screen back to the starting message!

story.title: Sensor Discovery
story.narrative: Chip discovers he has a hidden motion sensor inside. What happens when you shake him?

create.title: Motion Detector
create.prompt: Build a motion detector that reacts when the device is moved. What should happen?

explore.title: Read the Sensor
explore.instructions: Use IMU blocks to detect shaking and tilting. Display the sensor values on screen!
explore.device_interaction: IMU reads your movements. Display shows live sensor data.
explore.simulator_fallback: Click to simulate shaking and tilting.

celebrate.title: Sensor Explorer!
celebrate.summary: You read data from a real sensor! Sensors let computers feel the physical world.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child learned to read sensor data (accelerometer) and use it to trigger program behavior.

title: Counting Shakes
description: Use a variable to count how many times the M5Stick is shaken.
story_text: Chip wants to keep score! "That was SO fun! But wait -- how many times did you shake me? I lost count! I bet you can teach me to COUNT my shakes. We'll need a special thing called a variable -- it's like a box that remembers a number for us!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
count = 0
M5.Lcd.drawString("Shakes: 0", 20, 60, 0x00FF00)
while True:
    M5.update()
    if shake_detected():
        count = count + 1
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("Shakes:", 30, 40, 0xFFFFFF)
        M5.Lcd.drawString(str(count), 50, 80, 0x00FF00)
        M5.Speaker.tone(660, 100)
        time.sleep(0.3)
    time.sleep(0.1)

hints.text: You need a variable to keep score! Go to the Variables category and create a new variable called "count". Set it to 0 at the start.
Inside your Forever loop, when a shake is detected, use the Change Variable block to add 1 to your count variable.
After changing the count, clear the screen and display the count number. Use a Display Text block to show "Shakes:" and another to show the count value!

story.title: Shake Counter
story.narrative: Chip wants to count how many times you shake him. Variables to the rescue!

create.title: Shake Game
create.prompt: Build a game: how many shakes in 10 seconds? Add a countdown and high score!

explore.title: Build a Counter
explore.instructions: Use a variable to count shakes. Each shake adds 1. Display the count on screen!
explore.device_interaction: IMU detects shakes. Display shows running count. Buzzer beeps per shake.
explore.simulator_fallback: Click to shake and see the counter increment.

celebrate.title: Counter Coder!
celebrate.summary: You used VARIABLES to remember and count! Variables are how computers store information.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child learned about variables (storing and updating data) through a physical shake-counting program.

title: Dice Roller
description: Shake to roll a random number from 1 to 6 like a real dice.
story_text: Chip wants to play a board game! "I love board games but there's one problem -- I can't find the dice! Wait... what if YOU turn me into a dice? Shake me and I'll show a random number from 1 to 6! It's like MAGIC but it's actually MATH!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Shake to roll!", 10, 50, 0xFFFFFF)
while True:
    M5.update()
    if shake_detected():
        roll = random.randint(1, 6)
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x0000FF)
        M5.Lcd.drawString(str(roll), 55, 50, 0xFFFFFF)
        M5.Speaker.tone(440, 300)
        time.sleep(1.0)
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("Shake to roll!", 10, 50, 0xFFFFFF)
    time.sleep(0.1)

hints.text: You need a random number! Look in the Math category for a Random Integer block. Set it to pick between 1 and 6.
When you detect a shake, save the random number in a variable called "roll", then display it on the screen nice and big!
Inside the If shake block: set roll to random 1-6, clear screen blue, show the roll number, play a tone, wait 1 second, then reset the screen to show "Shake to roll!" again.

story.title: Random Roller
story.narrative: Chip needs a dice for a board game but only has his M5Stick. Can code make random numbers?

create.title: My Random Game
create.prompt: Build a game that uses random numbers. A spinner? A fortune teller? A random color picker?

explore.device_interaction: Shake triggers random number. Display shows dice face. Buzzer plays roll sound.

title: Magic 8-Ball
description: Build a fortune-telling Magic 8-Ball that gives random answers when shaken.
story_text: Chip wants to predict the future! "I've always wanted to be a fortune teller! You know those Magic 8-Balls? Ask a question, shake, and get an answer! We can use random numbers to pick a different answer each time. Shake me and I'll tell your FORTUNE!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>

hints.text: Start with a mystery-looking screen! Use a dark blue background and show "Ask me anything!" to invite questions.
When shaken, pick a random number 1 to 6. Use If blocks to show a different answer for each number -- like "Yes!", "No way!", or "Maybe..."
Add 6 If blocks, one for each answer (1=Yes!, 2=No way!, 3=Maybe..., 4=Ask again!, 5=Totally!, 6=Nope!). Play a spooky low tone (220) and wait 2 seconds before resetting!

story.title: Fortune Teller
story.narrative: Chip wants to predict the future! Build a Magic 8-Ball that gives random answers when shaken.

explore.title: Build the 8-Ball

explore.device_interaction: Shake to get random answer on display. Buzzer plays mystical sound.
explore.simulator_fallback: Click to shake and see random answers.

celebrate.title: Magic Coder!
celebrate.summary: You built a complete app: input (shake), processing (random), output (display). That is real programming!
celebrate.parent_note: Your child combined multiple concepts (events, randomness, conditionals, display) into a complete application.

title: My First Piano
description: Use the two buttons to play different musical notes like a tiny piano.
story_text: Chip wants to be a musician! "I've been watching music videos and I REALLY want to play piano! I have two buttons and a speaker -- that's almost like a piano, right? If Button A plays one note and Button B plays another, we can make music! Let's rock!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Piano!", 35, 20, 0xFFFFFF)
M5.Lcd.drawString("A = Do", 20, 80, 0xFF0000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("B = Mi", 20, 110, 0x0000FF)
while True:
    M5.update()
    if M5.BtnA.isPressed():
        M5.Speaker.tone(262, 300)
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0xFF0000)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("Do!", 50, 60, 0xFFFFFF)
        time.sleep(0.3)
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("Piano!", 35, 20, 0xFFFFFF)
    if M5.BtnB.isPressed():
        M5.Speaker.tone(330, 300)
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x0000FF)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("Mi!", 50, 60, 0xFFFFFF)
        time.sleep(0.3)
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("Piano!", 35, 20, 0xFFFFFF)
    time.sleep(0.05)

hints.text: Start with a Forever loop and check for button presses inside it. Use If blocks with "Button A pressed?" and "Button B pressed?" conditions.
When Button A is pressed, play a tone at 262 (that's the note Do!). When Button B is pressed, play a tone at 330 (that's Mi!).
Make it visual too! When A is pressed, turn the screen red and show "Do!". When B is pressed, turn it blue and show "Mi!". Add a short wait (0.3s) after each note.

story.title: Piano Keys

explore.simulator_fallback: Click virtual buttons to play notes.

celebrate.title: Music Coder!
celebrate.summary: You turned buttons into piano keys with code! Conditionals make buttons do different things.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child used conditional logic (if-then) to create an interactive musical instrument.

title: Song Player
description: Program a sequence of notes to play Twinkle Twinkle Little Star.
story_text: Chip learned single notes! "One note is fun but a SONG is better! Do you know Twinkle Twinkle Little Star? The first part goes: do do so so la la so! Each note is just a number. Let's program the whole first line!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Twinkle", 30, 30, 0xFFFF00)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Twinkle!", 25, 60, 0xFFFF00)
time.sleep(1.0)
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000080)
M5.Lcd.drawString("C", 60, 60, 0xFFFFFF)
M5.Speaker.tone(262, 400)
time.sleep(0.5)
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000080)
M5.Lcd.drawString("C", 60, 60, 0xFFFFFF)
M5.Speaker.tone(262, 400)
time.sleep(0.5)
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x800080)
M5.Lcd.drawString("G", 60, 60, 0xFFFFFF)
M5.Speaker.tone(392, 400)
time.sleep(0.5)
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x800080)
M5.Lcd.drawString("G", 60, 60, 0xFFFFFF)
M5.Speaker.tone(392, 400)
time.sleep(0.5)
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x008000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("A", 60, 60, 0xFFFFFF)
M5.Speaker.tone(440, 400)
time.sleep(0.5)
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x008000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("A", 60, 60, 0xFFFFFF)
M5.Speaker.tone(440, 400)
time.sleep(0.5)
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x800000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("G", 60, 60, 0xFFFFFF)
M5.Speaker.tone(392, 800)
time.sleep(1.0)
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Great song!", 20, 60, 0x00FF00)

hints.text: A song is just notes played one after another! Use Play Tone blocks with Wait blocks between them. C=262, G=392, A=440.
The pattern for Twinkle Twinkle is: C C G G A A G (hold). Play each note for 400ms with a 0.5 second wait between notes.
Play tone 262 twice, then 392 twice, then 440 twice, then 392 once (longer, 800ms). Change the screen color for each different note to make it visual!

story.title: Jukebox
story.narrative: Chip wants to play songs automatically. Use loops and lists to program a melody!

create.title: My Song
create.prompt: Program your favorite simple song or compose an original one. Add tempo changes for expression!

explore.title: Program a Song
explore.instructions: Create a list of notes with durations. Use a loop to play them in sequence. Program a complete melody!
explore.device_interaction: Buzzer plays your programmed melody. Display shows the notes as they play.
explore.simulator_fallback: Hear your melody through browser audio.

celebrate.title: Song Programmer!
celebrate.summary: You programmed a complete song! Lists and loops are powerful tools for creating sequences.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child used lists (arrays) and loops to program sequential melodies, learning data structure basics.

title: Sound Effects Machine
description: Use loops to create rising and falling sound effects with the buttons.
story_text: Chip wants video game sounds! "You know those cool sounds in video games? Like when you get a power-up -- BWEEEOOP! Or when you lose -- bwoooop... Button A can be POWER UP with sounds going higher and higher, and Button B can be POWER DOWN going lower! Let's make a sound effects machine!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
M5.Lcd.drawString("Sound FX!", 25, 30, 0xFFFFFF)
M5.Lcd.drawString("A = Up", 20, 80, 0x00FF00)
M5.Lcd.drawString("B = Down", 20, 110, 0xFF0000)
while True:
    M5.update()
    if M5.BtnA.isPressed():
        for i in range(8):
            M5.Speaker.tone(200 + i * 100, 80)
            M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x00FF00)
            time.sleep(0.1)
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("POWER UP!", 20, 60, 0x00FF00)
        time.sleep(0.5)
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("Sound FX!", 25, 30, 0xFFFFFF)
    if M5.BtnB.isPressed():
        for i in range(8):
            M5.Speaker.tone(900 - i * 100, 80)
            M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0xFF0000)
            time.sleep(0.1)
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("POWER DOWN!", 10, 60, 0xFF0000)
        time.sleep(0.5)
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("Sound FX!", 25, 30, 0xFFFFFF)
    time.sleep(0.05)

hints.text: A power-up sound goes from low to high! Use a Repeat loop with 8 steps. Each step plays a tone that gets higher.
Start the tone at 200 and add 100 each time through the loop. So: 200, 300, 400... up to 900! Use a short duration like 80ms for each beep.
For Button A (power up): loop 8 times playing tone (200 + i*100) for 80ms. For Button B (power down): loop 8 times playing tone (900 - i*100). Flash the screen green or red!

celebrate.parent_note: Your child combined conditional logic, sound generation, and user interface design into a creative application.

title: Tilt-o-Meter
description: Read the accelerometer to show which way the M5Stick is tilting.
story_text: Chip feels tipsy! "Whoooa, when you tilt me I can FEEL it! My motion sensor tells me which way I'm leaning. It's like a seesaw! Tilt me left and I feel negative numbers, tilt me right and I feel positive numbers. Can you show my tilt on the screen? It'll be like a spirit level!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>

hints.text: The tilt sensor is in the Sensors category! Drag out the Read Accelerometer block to get the tilt values.
The X axis tells you left vs right tilt. Use If blocks to check: if tilt > 0.3 show "RIGHT", if tilt < -0.3 show "LEFT", otherwise show "LEVEL!"
Put everything in a Forever loop. Each time: clear screen, read accelerometer X value, then use 3 If blocks to show RIGHT (green), LEFT (red), or LEVEL (yellow)!

story.title: Level Up
story.narrative: Chip becomes a level tool! Use the IMU to measure tilt angles and display them.

explore.title: Build a Level
explore.instructions: Read IMU tilt values and display them as a visual level indicator. Tilt the device to test!
explore.device_interaction: IMU reads tilt. Display shows angle and level bubble.
explore.simulator_fallback: Drag to simulate tilting.

celebrate.title: Sensor Engineer!
celebrate.summary: You built a real measurement tool! Phones use the same sensor for screen rotation.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child created a practical sensor-based tool, learning about real-time data reading and visualization.

title: Balance Game
description: Tilt the M5Stick to keep a ball balanced in the center of the screen.
story_text: Chip wants to play balance! "I have an idea for a game! There's a ball on my screen and YOU have to keep it from falling off the edge by tilting me carefully! If you tilt too much, the ball rolls to the side. Can you keep it in the middle? Steady hands!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
ball_x = 67
ball_y = 120
while True:
    M5.update()
    accel = Imu.read_accel()
    M5.Lcd.fillCircle(ball_x, ball_y, 10, 0x000000)
    ball_x = ball_x + int(accel[0] * 5)
    if ball_x < 10:
        ball_x = 10
    if ball_x > 125:
        ball_x = 125
    M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
    M5.Lcd.drawString("Balance!", 30, 10, 0xFFFFFF)
    if ball_x < 20 or ball_x > 115:
        M5.Lcd.fillCircle(ball_x, ball_y, 10, 0xFF0000)
    else:
        M5.Lcd.fillCircle(ball_x, ball_y, 10, 0x00FF00)
    time.sleep(0.05)

hints.text: Start with a variable ball_x set to 67 (the middle of the screen). In a Forever loop, read the accelerometer and add the tilt to ball_x.
Multiply the tilt value by 5 to make the ball move faster. Add boundary checks so ball_x stays between 10 and 125 (the screen edges).
Draw the ball as a circle at (ball_x, 120). If ball_x is near the edge (less than 20 or more than 115), make the ball red. Otherwise green. Clear the screen each loop!

story.title: Stay Balanced!
story.narrative: Chip challenges you to keep a ball in the center of the screen by holding the device steady!

create.title: My Balance Game
create.prompt: Design a tilt-controlled game. Catch falling items? Navigate an obstacle course? Be creative!

explore.title: Build the Balance Game
explore.instructions: Map IMU tilt to a ball position on screen. Keep the ball in the center zone to score points!
explore.device_interaction: IMU tilt moves ball. Display shows ball and target zone. Buzzer alerts when off-center.
explore.simulator_fallback: Click and drag to simulate tilting.

celebrate.summary: You built a real game with sensor input! Game developers use the same techniques.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child built an interactive game combining sensor input, game logic, scoring, and visual feedback.

title: Tilt Maze
description: Navigate a dot through a maze by tilting the M5Stick to reach the goal.
story_text: Chip is stuck in a maze! "HELP! I'm trapped in a maze! There are walls everywhere and I need to find the exit! The exit is the green square. Can you tilt me carefully to move the dot through the maze? Don't hit the walls! This is like a real video game!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field></block></xml>
solution_code:
dot_x = 15
dot_y = 30
goal_x = 100
goal_y = 200
while True:
    M5.update()
    accel = Imu.read_accel()
    M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
    M5.Lcd.fillRect(0, 80, 90, 8, 0x808080)
    M5.Lcd.fillRect(45, 150, 90, 8, 0x808080)
    M5.Lcd.fillRect(goal_x, goal_y, 20, 20, 0x00FF00)
    dot_x = dot_x + int(accel[0] * 3)
    dot_y = dot_y + int(accel[1] * 3)
    if dot_x < 5:
        dot_x = 5
    if dot_x > 130:
        dot_x = 130
    if dot_y < 5:
        dot_y = 5
    if dot_y > 235:
        dot_y = 235
    M5.Lcd.fillCircle(dot_x, dot_y, 5, 0xFFFF00)
    if dot_x > goal_x and dot_x < goal_x + 20 and dot_y > goal_y and dot_y < goal_y + 20:
        M5.Lcd.fillScreen(0x00FF00)
        M5.Lcd.drawString("YOU WIN!", 25, 60, 0xFFFFFF)
        M5.Speaker.tone(880, 500)
        time.sleep(3.0)
        dot_x = 15
        dot_y = 30
    time.sleep(0.05)

hints.text: You need two variables: dot_x and dot_y for the ball position. Read the accelerometer and use both X and Y values to move the dot in two directions!
Draw gray rectangles as walls and a green rectangle as the goal. Move the dot by adding accel[0]*3 to dot_x and accel[1]*3 to dot_y.
Add boundary checks (5 to 130 for X, 5 to 235 for Y). Check if the dot is inside the green goal square. If it is, show "YOU WIN!", play a victory tone, and reset the dot position!

story.title: Maze Navigator

explore.device_interaction: Tilt navigates through maze on display. Buzzer on wall hit. Celebration on reaching exit.
explore.simulator_fallback: Use arrow keys to navigate the maze.

celebrate.title: Game Master!
celebrate.summary: You built a complete tilt-controlled maze game! You combined sensors, graphics, logic, and loops.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child built a complex project combining multiple programming concepts into a playable game.

title: Count to 20
description: Practice counting objects from 1 to 20! Build a counting machine on your M5Stick that displays numbers one by one with a beep for each count.
story_text: Hi friend! I love counting things. Let's count together! Tap each one and see how high we can go. Ready? One, two, three... go!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_text" x="20" y="20"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">Count: 1</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Count to 20!", 10, 10)

for i in range(1, 21):
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 50, 135, 40, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(i), 50, 60)
    Speaker.tone(440 + i * 20, 100)
    time.sleep(0.5)

Lcd.drawString("Done!", 40, 100)

hints.text: Start by showing a title on the screen with the Display Text block.
Use a Loop block to count from 1 to 20. Set the repeat count to 20.
Inside the loop, use Display Number to show the current count.
Add a Buzzer Tone block inside the loop so each number gets a beep!
Add a Wait block (0.5 seconds) so you can see each number before it changes.

questions.hint: Tap each star one by one: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5!
questions.prompt: Count the stars!

questions.hint: Count slowly. There are 8 apples!
questions.prompt: Count the apples!

questions.hint: Count past 10! 10, 11, 12. There are 12!
questions.prompt: How many butterflies do you see?

questions.hint: Keep going after 10! 11, 12, 13, 14, 15.
questions.prompt: Count all the fish!

questions.hint: Almost to 20! Count carefully: 15, 16, 17, 18.
questions.prompt: Can you count all the flowers?

questions.hint: You can do it! Count all the way to 20!
questions.prompt: Count every single heart!

title: Count to 50
description: Keep counting higher! Build a program that counts up to 50 on your M5Stick, showing numbers on the display with fun sound effects.
story_text: Wow, you can count to 20! But Chip knows you can go even BIGGER! Let's count all the way to 50. That's a LOT of things. You got this!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_number" x="20" y="20"><value name="NUMBER"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">1</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">40</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Count to 50!", 10, 10)

for i in range(1, 51):
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 50, 135, 40, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(i), 40, 60)
    if i % 10 == 0:
        Speaker.tone(880, 200)
    else:
        Speaker.tone(440, 50)
    time.sleep(0.3)

Lcd.drawString("50!", 50, 100)

hints.text: Use Display Text to show a title like Count to 50 at the top.
Use a Loop block set to repeat 50 times to count each number.
Inside the loop, display the current number with Display Number.
Try making a special sound every 10 numbers using an If block!
Add a short Wait (0.3 seconds) between numbers so they don't flash too fast.

questions.hint: Count past 20! 20, 21, 22.

questions.hint: Keep counting! 28, 29, 30!
questions.prompt: How many rockets?

questions.hint: You are almost there! 38, 39, 40!

title: Count to 100!
description: Reach the big number! Build a counting program that goes all the way to 100. Make the M5Stick celebrate with lights and sounds at every milestone!
story_text: You are a counting champion! Chip is SO proud. Now let's try something amazing -- counting to ONE HUNDRED! That is the biggest number yet. Let's go!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="controls_repeat_ext" x="20" y="20"><value name="TIMES"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">100</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Count to 100!", 5, 10)

for i in range(1, 101):
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 50, 135, 40, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(i), 40, 60)
    if i % 10 == 0:
        Speaker.tone(880, 300)
        Power.setLed(50)
        time.sleep(0.3)
        Power.setLed(0)
    else:
        time.sleep(0.2)

Speaker.tone(1000, 500)
Lcd.drawString("100! Yay!", 20, 100)

hints.text: Start with a Display Text block to show your title at the top.
Use a Loop block with 100 repeats to count every number.
Display each number using Display Number inside the loop.
Use an If block to check if the number is a multiple of 10 for a special celebration!
Turn on the LED and play a high-pitched tone at every 10th number.

questions.hint: Count past 50! 53, 54, 55.
questions.prompt: Count the gems!

questions.hint: You can do it! Keep going past 70.
questions.prompt: Count all the leaves!

questions.hint: What comes right after 59?
What comes right after 79?

title: Number Before & After
description: Find the number neighbors! Build a number quiz on your M5Stick that shows a number and you guess what comes before and after it.
story_text: Every number has neighbors! The number before 5 is 4, and the number after 5 is 6. Let's find all the number neighbors together. Chip loves number neighbors!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_random_number" x="20" y="20"><value name="MIN"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">2</field></shadow></value><value name="MAX"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">19</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Number Neighbors", 5, 5)

for round in range(5):
    num = random.randint(2, 19)
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 90, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString("Before: " + str(num - 1), 10, 40)
    Lcd.drawString("-> " + str(num) + " <-", 30, 65)
    Lcd.drawString("After: " + str(num + 1), 10, 90)
    Speaker.tone(660, 150)
    time.sleep(2)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 90, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Great job!", 25, 60)

hints.text: Use the Random Number block to pick a number between 2 and 19.
Display the number in the middle of the screen with Display Text.
Use Math blocks to calculate number - 1 (before) and number + 1 (after).
Show all three numbers: the one before, the number, and the one after.
Put it in a loop to practice with 5 different numbers!

questions.hint: What comes right before 5? Count: 3, 4, 5.

What comes right before 12?
What number is between 17 and 19?
What comes right after 15?
3 plus 1 is 4!

title: Comparing Numbers
description: Which number is bigger? Which is smaller? Build a comparing game on your M5Stick that shows two numbers and you decide!
story_text: Chip has a question for you! If you had 3 cookies or 7 cookies, which is MORE? Let's learn to compare numbers and figure out which one is bigger or smaller!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_number" x="20" y="20"><value name="NUMBER"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">5</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value><next><block type="m5_display_number"><value name="NUMBER"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">8</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">80</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Compare!", 30, 5)

for round in range(5):
    a = random.randint(1, 20)
    b = random.randint(1, 20)
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(a), 20, 50)
    Lcd.drawString(str(b), 90, 50)
    time.sleep(1)
    if a > b:
        Lcd.drawString(str(a) + " > " + str(b), 20, 85)
    elif a < b:
        Lcd.drawString(str(a) + " < " + str(b), 20, 85)
    else:
        Lcd.drawString(str(a) + " = " + str(b), 20, 85)
    Speaker.tone(660, 150)
    time.sleep(2)

Lcd.drawString("Great job!", 25, 110)

hints.text: Pick two random numbers using the Random Number block.
Display both numbers on the screen side by side using Display Number.
Use an If block to check which number is bigger with the compare block.
Show the > or < or = symbol between the numbers.
Play a sound when the answer is revealed!

questions.hint: Count up: 3, 4, 5, 6, 7. You counted higher to get to 7!
questions.prompt: Which number is BIGGER: 3 or 7?

questions.hint: 4 comes before 9 when you count, so 4 is smaller.

title: Count by 10s to 100
description: Skip count by 10s! Build a program that counts 10, 20, 30... all the way to 100 with a big celebration sound at each step.
story_text: Want to count SUPER fast? Chip will show you a trick! Instead of counting 1, 2, 3... we can SKIP and count by 10s! 10, 20, 30... all the way to 100! Let's try!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="controls_repeat_ext" x="20" y="20"><value name="TIMES"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><next><block type="m5_display_text"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">100!</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">40</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">100</field></shadow></value></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Count by 10s!", 10, 10)

for i in range(1, 11):
    num = i * 10
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 50, 135, 40, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(num), 40, 60)
    Speaker.tone(440 + i * 50, 200)
    Power.setLed(30)
    time.sleep(0.3)
    Power.setLed(0)
    time.sleep(0.5)

Speaker.tone(1000, 500)
Lcd.drawString("100! Done!", 20, 100)

Flash the LED with each count to make it more exciting!

title: Ordering Numbers
description: Put numbers in order! Build a program that shows random numbers and then sorts them from smallest to biggest on the display.
story_text: Chip mixed up some numbers and needs your help! Can you put them back in order from the smallest to the biggest? It is like lining up from shortest to tallest!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_text" x="20" y="20"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">Order Numbers!</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Order Numbers!", 5, 5)

nums = [random.randint(1, 20) for _ in range(4)]
Lcd.drawString("Mixed up:", 10, 30)
Lcd.drawString(" ".join(str(n) for n in nums), 10, 50)
time.sleep(2)

nums.sort()
Speaker.tone(660, 200)
Lcd.drawString("In order:", 10, 80)
Lcd.drawString(" ".join(str(n) for n in nums), 10, 100)
Speaker.tone(880, 300)

hints.text: Show a title at the top of the screen with Display Text.
Pick 4 random numbers and display them in mixed-up order.
Wait a moment so the player can see the mixed-up numbers.
Sort the numbers from smallest to biggest and show them again.
Play a celebration sound when the sorted answer is revealed!

questions.prompt: Put these numbers in order from smallest to biggest!
Order these from smallest to biggest!

title: Adding to 5
description: Learn to add small numbers together! Build an addition quiz on your M5Stick where all answers are 5 or less.
story_text: Chip is baking cookies and needs your help! If you have 2 cookies and bake 1 more, how many do you have? That is called ADDING! Let's practice adding little numbers together.
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_text" x="20" y="20"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">1 + 2 = ?</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Add to 5!", 25, 5)

for round in range(6):
    a = random.randint(0, 5)
    b = random.randint(0, 5 - a)
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 80, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(a) + " + " + str(b) + " = ?", 20, 50)
    time.sleep(1.5)
    Lcd.drawString(str(a) + " + " + str(b) + " = " + str(a+b), 15, 80)
    Speaker.tone(880, 200)
    time.sleep(1.5)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 80, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("You can add!", 15, 60)

hints.text: Pick two random numbers that add up to 5 or less.
Show the problem on screen like 1 + 2 = ? using Display Text.
Wait so the player can think about the answer.
Use Math Addition to calculate the answer and display it.
Play a happy sound when the answer appears!

title: Adding to 10
description: Add bigger numbers! Build an addition quiz where all answers are 10 or less. Watch the numbers add up on your M5Stick!
story_text: You are getting SO good at adding! Now let's try bigger numbers. Chip's friend needs help counting how many toys they have together. Can you add them up?
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_random_number" x="20" y="20"><value name="MIN"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">1</field></shadow></value><value name="MAX"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">9</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)

    b = random.randint(1, 10 - a)
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 85, 0x000000)

    Speaker.tone(880, 200)
    time.sleep(1.5)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 85, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("10 star!", 30, 60)

title: Take Away (Subtract to 5)
description: Learn to take away! Build a subtraction game on your M5Stick where you start with a small number and remove some.
story_text: Oh no! Some of Chip's snacks got eaten. If Chip had 5 crackers and ate 2, how many are left? That is called SUBTRACTING or taking away. Let's practice!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_text" x="20" y="20"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">5 - 2 = ?</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Take Away!", 25, 5)

for round in range(6):
    a = random.randint(1, 5)
    b = random.randint(0, a)
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 80, 0x000000)
    for i in range(a):
        Lcd.fillCircle(15 + i * 22, 45, 8, 0x22C55E)
    time.sleep(1)
    for i in range(b):
        Lcd.fillCircle(15 + (a - 1 - i) * 22, 45, 8, 0x333333)
        Speaker.tone(300, 100)
        time.sleep(0.3)
    Lcd.drawString(str(a) + " - " + str(b) + " = " + str(a-b), 15, 75)
    Speaker.tone(660, 200)
    time.sleep(1.5)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 80, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Subtract pro!", 10, 60)

hints.text: Pick a starting number from 1 to 5 and draw that many circles.
Pick how many to take away (less than or equal to the start number).
Turn the taken-away circles dark to show they are gone.
Display the subtraction equation with the answer below.
Play a sound effect as each circle disappears!

description: Subtract bigger numbers! Build a visual subtraction game starting with up to 10 objects on your M5Stick.
story_text: Great job taking away small numbers! Now let's try bigger ones. Chip has 10 stickers but gave some to friends. How many does Chip have left? Let's figure it out!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_circle" x="20" y="20"><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">50</field></shadow></value><value name="RADIUS"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">6</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Subtract 10!", 15, 5)

for round in range(5):
    a = random.randint(3, 10)
    b = random.randint(1, a)
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 90, 0x000000)
    for i in range(a):
        Lcd.fillCircle(10 + i * 13, 45, 5, 0x3B82F6)
    time.sleep(1)
    for i in range(b):
        Lcd.fillCircle(10 + (a - 1 - i) * 13, 45, 5, 0x333333)
        Speaker.tone(250, 80)
        time.sleep(0.3)
    Lcd.drawString(str(a) + " - " + str(b) + " = " + str(a-b), 10, 70)
    Speaker.tone(660, 200)
    time.sleep(2)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 90, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Great math!", 20, 60)

hints.text: Pick a random number from 3 to 10 and draw that many small circles.
Pick how many to subtract (between 1 and the start number).
Make the subtracted circles go dark one by one with a sound.
Show the full equation below: start - taken = left.
Put it in a loop for multiple practice rounds!

title: Making 10
description: Find pairs of numbers that add up to 10! Build a Friends of 10 display that shows all the ways to make 10.
story_text: Chip's favorite number is 10! Did you know lots of number pairs add up to 10? Like 3 and 7, or 6 and 4. These are called "friends of 10." Let's find them all!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_text" x="20" y="20"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">0 + 10 = 10</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Making 10!", 25, 5)

for i in range(11):
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 85, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(i) + " + " + str(10-i) + " = 10", 10, 50)
    bar_w = int(i * 11.5)
    Lcd.fillRect(5, 80, bar_w, 10, 0x3B82F6)
    Lcd.fillRect(5 + bar_w, 80, int((10-i) * 11.5), 10, 0xF97316)
    Speaker.tone(400 + i * 40, 100)
    time.sleep(0.8)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 85, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("All pairs!", 25, 60)
Speaker.tone(880, 400)

hints.text: Use a Loop from 0 to 10 to show every pair that makes 10.
For each number i, its partner is 10 minus i.
Display the equation: i + (10-i) = 10 on screen.
Draw colored bars showing how much each number contributes.
Play rising tones as the first number gets bigger!

title: Story Problems
description: Solve word problems about adding and taking away! Build a story problem display on your M5Stick with visual aids.
story_text: Chip loves stories and math! Let's put them together. Read each little story and figure out the answer. You can do this!

solution_code:

    ("3 cats + 2 cats", 5),

    time.sleep(1.5)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Story solver!", 10, 60)

title: Tens and Ones
description: Learn that numbers are made of tens and ones! Build a place value display on your M5Stick showing how 13 is 1 ten and 3 ones.
story_text: Chip has a secret about numbers! Big numbers are made of TENS and ONES. The number 14 is 1 group of ten and 4 extra ones. Cool, right? Let's explore!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_text" x="20" y="20"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">Tens  Ones</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Tens & Ones!", 15, 5)

for round in range(5):
    num = random.randint(11, 29)
    tens = num // 10
    ones = num % 10
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(num), 55, 30)
    Lcd.drawString(str(tens) + " ten", 10, 60)
    Lcd.drawString(str(ones) + " ones", 70, 60)
    Lcd.fillRect(10, 80, tens * 40, 15, 0x3B82F6)
    for o in range(ones):
        Lcd.fillRect(70 + o * 8, 80, 6, 15, 0xF97316)
    Speaker.tone(550, 200)
    time.sleep(2.5)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Place value!", 15, 60)

hints.text: Pick a number between 11 and 29 to practice with.
Divide by 10 to find how many tens. The remainder is the ones.

title: Count by 10s with Beads
description: Use the bead frame to count groups of 10! Build a visual bead counter on your M5Stick.
story_text: Look at this cool bead frame! Each row has 10 beads. Chip will help you see how groups of 10 make bigger numbers. Slide the beads and count!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="controls_repeat_ext" x="20" y="20"><value name="TIMES"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Count by 10s!", 10, 5)

for i in range(1, 11):
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
    for b in range(i):
        row = b // 5
        col = b % 5
        Lcd.fillCircle(15 + col * 25, 45 + row * 20, 8, 0xEF4444)
    Lcd.drawString(str(i * 10), 45, 95)
    Speaker.tone(400 + i * 40, 150)
    time.sleep(0.8)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("100!", 50, 60)
Speaker.tone(880, 400)

title: Compare Numbers (>, <, =)
description: Use greater than, less than, and equal signs to compare numbers! Build a comparison quiz on your M5Stick.
story_text: Time to learn some fun math symbols! The > sign means "greater than" and the < sign means "less than." Think of them like a hungry alligator -- it always eats the BIGGER number! Let's try!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_random_number" x="20" y="20"><value name="MIN"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">1</field></shadow></value><value name="MAX"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Compare!", 30, 5)

for round in range(6):
    a = random.randint(1, 20)
    b = random.randint(1, 20)
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(a), 20, 45)
    Lcd.drawString("?", 60, 45)
    Lcd.drawString(str(b), 95, 45)
    time.sleep(1.5)
    if a > b:
        sign = ">"
    elif a < b:
        sign = "<"
    else:
        sign = "="
    Lcd.fillRect(50, 35, 25, 25, 0x000000)
    Lcd.setTextColor(0x22C55E, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(sign, 60, 45)
    Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(a) + " " + sign + " " + str(b), 15, 80)
    Speaker.tone(660, 200)
    time.sleep(1.5)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Compare pro!", 10, 60)

hints.text: Pick two random numbers to compare.
Display both numbers with a question mark between them.
Use If blocks to check: is the first number bigger, smaller, or equal?
Replace the ? with >, <, or = in green.
Show the full comparison below and play a sound!

title: Number Patterns
description: Find the pattern and figure out what comes next! Build a pattern quiz on your M5Stick that shows number sequences.
story_text: Chip loves puzzles! Numbers can make cool patterns. Like 2, 4, 6, 8 -- see how they go up by 2 each time? Let's find more patterns together!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_text" x="20" y="20"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">2 4 6 ?</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">15</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Patterns!", 30, 5)

patterns = [
    ([2,4,6,8], 10, "+2"),
    ([5,10,15,20], 25, "+5"),
    ([1,3,5,7], 9, "+2"),
    ([10,20,30,40], 50, "+10"),
    ([3,6,9,12], 15, "+3")
]

for nums, answer, rule in patterns:
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
    display = " ".join(str(n) for n in nums) + " ?"
    Lcd.drawString(display, 5, 45)
    Lcd.drawString("Rule: " + rule, 25, 70)
    time.sleep(2)
    Lcd.drawString("-> " + str(answer) + "!", 35, 90)
    Speaker.tone(770, 200)
    time.sleep(1.5)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Pattern pro!", 15, 60)

hints.text: Show a number sequence with a question mark at the end: 2 4 6 ?
Display the rule below (like +2 or +5) as a hint.

title: Attributes of Shapes
description: Count sides and corners! Build a shape attribute quiz on your M5Stick that shows shapes with their properties.
story_text: Each shape has special things about it -- sides and corners! A triangle has 3 sides and 3 corners. Let's count the sides and corners of different shapes. Chip is great at counting!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_rect" x="20" y="20"><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">45</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">35</field></shadow></value><value name="WIDTH"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">45</field></shadow></value><value name="HEIGHT"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">45</field></shadow></value><next><block type="m5_display_text"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">4 sides 4 corners</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">5</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">95</field></shadow></value></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Shape Facts!", 15, 5)

shapes = [
    ("Square", 0xEF4444, "4 sides, 4 corners"),
    ("Circle", 0x3B82F6, "0 sides, 0 corners"),
    ("Rectangle", 0x22C55E, "4 sides, 4 corners"),
]

for name, color, facts in shapes:
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
    if name == "Circle":
        Lcd.fillCircle(67, 55, 18, color)
    elif name == "Square":
        Lcd.fillRect(47, 37, 36, 36, color)
    else:
        Lcd.fillRect(27, 42, 80, 26, color)
    Lcd.drawString(name, 35, 80)
    time.sleep(1)
    Lcd.drawString(facts, 5, 100)
    Speaker.tone(660, 200)
    time.sleep(2)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)

title: 3D Shapes
description: Explore shapes you can hold! Build a 3D shape gallery on your M5Stick using circles and rectangles to represent cubes, spheres, and cylinders.
story_text: Flat shapes are cool, but 3D shapes are even cooler because you can HOLD them! A ball is a sphere, a box is a cube, and a can of soup is a cylinder. Let's learn about 3D shapes!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_circle" x="20" y="20"><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">67</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">55</field></shadow></value><value name="RADIUS"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("3D Shapes!", 20, 5)

shapes_3d = [
    ("Sphere", "like a ball"),
    ("Cube", "like a box"),
    ("Cylinder", "like a can"),
    ("Cone", "like a hat")
]
colors = [0xEF4444, 0x3B82F6, 0x22C55E, 0xF97316]

for i, (name, desc) in enumerate(shapes_3d):
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
    if name == "Sphere":
        Lcd.fillCircle(67, 55, 20, colors[i])
    elif name == "Cube":
        Lcd.fillRect(47, 35, 40, 40, colors[i])
    elif name == "Cylinder":
        Lcd.fillRect(47, 35, 40, 40, colors[i])
        Lcd.drawCircle(67, 35, 20, colors[i])
    else:
        Lcd.fillRect(47, 45, 40, 30, colors[i])
    Lcd.drawString(name, 35, 85)
    Lcd.drawString(desc, 20, 100)
    Speaker.tone(440 + i * 60, 200)
    time.sleep(2)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 25, 135, 95, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("3D expert!", 20, 60)

title: Tell Time to the Hour
description: Learn to read a clock! Build a simple clock display on your M5Stick that shows times like 3:00 and 7:00.
story_text: Chip wants to know what time it is! A clock has a BIG hand and a LITTLE hand. When the big hand points UP to 12, the little hand shows the hour. Let's practice reading clocks!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_circle" x="20" y="20"><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">67</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value><value name="RADIUS"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">30</field></shadow></value></block></xml>
solution_code:
import math

Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x3B82F6, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("What Time?", 20, 5)

for hour in [3, 6, 9, 12]:
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 22, 135, 98, 0x000000)
    cx, cy, r = 67, 60, 30
    Lcd.drawCircle(cx, cy, r, 0xFFFFFF)
    Lcd.fillCircle(cx, cy, 2, 0xFFFFFF)
    angle = math.radians(hour * 30 - 90)
    hx = int(cx + 16 * math.cos(angle))
    hy = int(cy + 16 * math.sin(angle))
    Lcd.drawRect(cx, cy, hx-cx, hy-cy, 0x3B82F6)
    Lcd.drawRect(cx, cy, 0, -22, 0xEF4444)
    Lcd.drawString(str(hour) + ":00", 45, 100)
    Speaker.tone(500, 200)
    time.sleep(2.5)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 22, 135, 98, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Clock reader!", 10, 60)

hints.text: Draw a circle for the clock face.

left.text: Little hand on 3, big hand on 12

Little hand on 12, big hand on 12

questions.hint: The little hand tells the hour. It points to 5, so it is 5 o'clock!

questions.prompt: At any o'clock time, where does the BIG hand always point?

questions.hint: Most kids eat lunch around 12 o'clock, which is noon!
questions.prompt: Which time do you usually eat lunch?

title: Ending Sounds
description: Listen for the last sound in each word! Build a program that displays words and highlights the ending letter with a special sound.
story_text: You know the sounds at the START of words. Now let's listen to the END! Every word has a last sound. Chip will say a word, and you pick the letter that makes the ending sound. Let's go!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_text" x="20" y="20"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">cat</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">30</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">50</field></shadow></value><next><block type="m5_display_text"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">ends with: t</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">80</field></shadow></value></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x22C55E, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Ending Sounds!", 5, 5)

words = ["cat", "dog", "bus", "pen", "map", "fox"]
for word in words:
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 80, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(word, 40, 45)
    time.sleep(1)
    Lcd.setTextColor(0xF97316, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString("ends: " + word[-1], 25, 75)
    Lcd.setTextColor(0x22C55E, 0x000000)
    Speaker.tone(660, 200)
    time.sleep(1.3)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 80, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Sound expert!", 10, 60)

title: Sight Words Set 1
description: Learn five important words you see in every book: the, and, is, in, it! Build a sight word flashcard display on your M5Stick.
story_text: Some words show up ALL the time in books! Chip calls them sight words because you should know them on sight -- no sounding out needed! Today we learn: the, and, is, in, it. Ready?
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_text" x="20" y="20"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">the</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">40</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value><next><block type="m5_wait"><value name="SECONDS"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">2</field></shadow></value></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x22C55E, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Sight Words!", 10, 5)

words = ["the", "and", "is", "in", "it"]
colors = [0xEF4444, 0xF97316, 0xEAB308, 0x22C55E, 0x3B82F6]

for i in range(len(words)):
    Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 80, 0x000000)
    Lcd.setTextColor(colors[i], 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(words[i], 40, 55)
    Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(i+1) + "/5", 50, 90)
    Speaker.tone(500 + i * 50, 200)
    time.sleep(2)

Lcd.fillRect(0, 30, 135, 80, 0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x22C55E, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("All 5 words!", 15, 60)
Speaker.tone(880, 400)

back.text: THE - I see the dog.
AND - Red and blue.
IS - The cat is big.
IN - The toy is in the box.
IT - I like it!
