questions.hint: Tuba is biggest and deepest. Flute is small and high!
questions.prompt: Put these instruments from LOWEST to HIGHEST sounding!

questions.hint: The higher you go, the higher the note sounds!
questions.prompt: On a musical staff, where do HIGH notes live?

questions.hint: Mice make tiny, squeaky sounds way up high!
questions.prompt: A mouse squeak is an example of a ___ sound.

questions.hint: Starts with P!
questions.prompt: Which word describes how high or low a note sounds?

questions.hint: Moving UP means the pitch goes UP!
questions.prompt: When notes go UP on the staff, what happens?

title: Do Re Mi Fa Sol
description: Meet the musical staircase -- Do Re Mi Fa Sol! These special syllables help us sing any melody.
story_text: Chip is singing up the musical staircase! Do, Re, Mi, Fa, Sol -- each step goes a little higher. Let's climb the staircase together!

cards.back: Do is the HOME note -- the starting note! Think of it as the ground floor.
Re is the second step up! A little higher than Do.
Mi is the third step! Bright and cheerful.
Fa is the fourth step. Almost halfway up!
Sol is the fifth step -- strong and bright! Sol and Do are best friends in music.

activities.prompt: Flip each card to learn the solfege notes!
Match each solfege note to its position!

questions.hint: The home note starts the staircase!
questions.prompt: Which solfege note is the HOME note?

questions.hint: Do is the lowest!
questions.prompt: From lowest to highest: Mi, Do, Re. Which comes FIRST?

questions.hint: Count: Do, Re, Mi, Fa, Sol!
questions.prompt: How many solfege notes did we learn?

questions.hint: Sol is at the top of our five-step staircase!
questions.prompt: Which solfege note is the HIGHEST we learned?

title: AB Song Form
description: Discover how songs are built with two different sections -- A for the verse and B for the chorus!
story_text: Chip just noticed something AWESOME about songs! Most songs have parts that are DIFFERENT from each other. Musicians call the verse "A" and the chorus "B". So a song goes A-B-A-B! It's like a pattern!

cards.back: The A section tells the story and words often change each time.
The B section is the catchy part that stays the SAME every time!
AB form means two different sections. A-B-A-B is a musical pattern!

activities.prompt: Flip to learn about song sections!

questions.hint: A comes first, just like the verse!
questions.prompt: In AB form, what does A usually stand for?

questions.hint: The catchy part everyone sings along to!
questions.prompt: Which part stays the SAME every time?

questions.hint: Verse = A, Chorus = B!
questions.prompt: A song goes: Verse, Chorus, Verse, Chorus. What is the pattern?

questions.hint: Letters help us see the pattern!
questions.prompt: Why do musicians use letters for song sections?

options.text: Because music is the same as spelling
To show which parts are the same and which are different
Because songs only have two notes
Because A and B are the only letters

right.text: Catchy part, words stay the same

activities.prompt: Match each song part to its description!

title: ABA Song Form
description: Find the musical sandwich! In ABA form, the first section comes BACK at the end.
story_text: Chip found a SANDWICH in music! Some songs start with Section A, go to Section B in the middle, and then Section A comes BACK again. A-B-A! It's like a sandwich: A is the bread, B is the filling!

questions.hint: ABA is like a sandwich!
questions.prompt: Put the sections of an ABA song in the right order!

questions.hint: Bread on top, filling in the middle, bread on bottom!
questions.prompt: Build a musical sandwich in ABA order!

questions.hint: Happy, then sad, then happy again!
questions.prompt: A song plays: happy melody, sad melody, happy melody again. Put it in order!

questions.hint: The last letter is A!
questions.prompt: In ABA form, what happens at the END?

options.text: A brand new section C plays

questions.hint: Bread-filling-bread = A-B-A!
questions.prompt: Why is ABA form like a sandwich?

options.text: Because music is made of food
Because A is on both sides with B in the middle
Because there are three slices of bread

questions.hint: ABA brings the first section BACK!
questions.prompt: What is the DIFFERENCE between AB and ABA form?

options.text: In ABA, Section A comes back at the end

questions.hint: Gentle (A), different (B), gentle again (A) = A-B-A!
questions.prompt: A lullaby starts gentle, gets louder, then goes back to gentle. What form?

questions.hint: Three parts but only TWO different sections!
questions.prompt: How many different sections does ABA form have?

title: Compose Your Own Melody
description: Use everything you learned to build a mini melody! Pick notes, arrange them, and become a real composer.
story_text: Chip is SO proud of you! You know about high and low, you can sing Do Re Mi, and you understand how songs are built. Now let's become a COMPOSER!

questions.hint: The fifth and highest note. Starts with S!
questions.prompt: The five solfege notes we learned are Do, Re, Mi, Fa, and ___.

questions.hint: Starts with C!
questions.prompt: A person who writes music is called a ___.

questions.hint: The very first note. Rhymes with go!
questions.prompt: The home note in solfege is called ___.

questions.hint: Look at A-B-A. Which letter is at the start AND the end?
questions.prompt: In ABA form, the letter ___ comes back at the end.

questions.hint: Starts with P!
questions.prompt: How high or low a note sounds is called its ___.

questions.hint: Start at the bottom with Do and climb up!
questions.prompt: Build a melody going UP! Lowest to highest.

questions.hint: Start at the top with Sol and step down!
questions.prompt: Build a melody going DOWN! Highest to lowest.

questions.hint: Musical sandwich! A first, B in the middle, A comes back!

items.text: Part A: Do Re Mi (going up)
Part B: Sol Fa Mi (going down)
Part A returns: Do Re Mi (going up again)

questions.prompt: Put together an ABA melody!

questions.hint: Do-Re-Mi goes UP, then Mi-Re-Do goes back DOWN!
questions.prompt: Your melody: Do, Re, Mi, Re, Do. What does it do?

options.text: It goes up and then comes back down
It stays on the same note

questions.hint: Ending on Do feels like coming home!
questions.prompt: Which is a good ending note for a melody?

options.text: Any note that is very loud
The fastest note you can play

title: Clap the Pattern
description: Learn to read and clap simple rhythm patterns! Feel the beat with your hands and body.
story_text: Hey rhythm maker! Chip here! Did you know you already make rhythms every day? When you clap your hands, stomp your feet, or tap on a table -- that's rhythm! Today we're going to read rhythm patterns and clap them out. Ready? Clap along with Chip!

questions.hint: Clap, clap, shhh, clap!
questions.prompt: Put these claps in the right rhythm pattern: CLAP CLAP REST CLAP!

questions.hint: One big clap, two quick claps, one more big clap, then silence!
questions.prompt: Arrange this 4-beat pattern: CLAP quick-quick CLAP REST

questions.hint: Quickest to longest!
questions.prompt: Build from SHORTEST to LONGEST notes!

questions.hint: Left-right-left-right!
questions.prompt: Put this marching rhythm in order: LEFT RIGHT LEFT RIGHT!

right.text: Make a sound on the beat

activities.prompt: Match each rhythm word to what it means!

title: Write Your Own Rhythm
description: Become a rhythm composer! Learn to write down rhythm patterns using note names and rests.
story_text: Chip is giving you a composer's notebook! Today YOU get to be the rhythm writer! We'll use note names to write patterns that add up to exactly 4 beats.

questions.hint: Quarter notes get one single beat.
questions.prompt: A quarter note lasts ___ beat(s).

questions.hint: Count: 1-2!
questions.prompt: A half note lasts ___ beats.

questions.hint: Half + half = 1 whole beat!
questions.prompt: Two eighth notes together last ___ beat(s) total.

questions.hint: 1 + 1 + ? = 4. You need 2 more beats.
questions.prompt: In a measure of 4 beats: Quarter + Quarter + ___ = 4 beats.

questions.hint: Matches the quarter note!
questions.prompt: A ___ rest means 1 beat of silence.

questions.hint: Three claps and then silence! 1+1+1+1=4.
questions.prompt: Build a 4-beat rhythm! Quarter, Quarter, Quarter, Quarter Rest
Write this 4-beat rhythm: Half note, two quick eighth notes, Quarter note

questions.hint: Two quick, one regular, two quick, then hold!
questions.prompt: Arrange: quick-quick, CLAP, quick-quick, HOLD!

title: Rhythm + Math: Counting Beats
description: Discover how music and math are best friends! Note values are like fractions.
story_text: Guess what, math whiz? Music IS math! Chip just figured out the secret: notes are like fractions! A whole note is like a whole pizza -- 4 slices. A half note is half -- 2 slices. A quarter note is one quarter -- 1 slice!

activities.prompt: Match each note to how many beats it gets!

questions.hint: 2+2=4 beats = 1 whole note!
questions.prompt: 2 half notes = ___ whole note(s).

questions.hint: 1+1+1+1=4 beats = 1 whole note!
questions.prompt: 4 quarter notes = ___ whole note(s).
1 half note + 2 quarter notes = ___ beats total.

questions.hint: Whole note = whole pizza = 4 beats!
questions.prompt: A quarter note is like 1 slice of a pizza that has ___ slices total.

questions.hint: Split a quarter in half!
questions.prompt: Half of a quarter note is an ___ note.

title: Body Percussion Band
description: Your body is an instrument! Learn to clap, snap, stomp, and pat your knees to make awesome rhythms.
story_text: Chip just discovered something AMAZING -- you don't need a drum or xylophone to make music. YOUR BODY is a percussion instrument! You can clap, snap, stomp, and pat your knees. Let's jam!

questions.hint: Stomp-clap-stomp-clap!
questions.prompt: Build the pattern: STOMP CLAP STOMP CLAP!

questions.hint: Pat-pat-clap-snap!
questions.prompt: Arrange: PAT PAT CLAP SNAP!

questions.hint: Start at the ground and work up!
questions.prompt: Put these body sounds from LOWEST to HIGHEST pitch!

questions.hint: BOOM BOOM CLAP (shhh)!
questions.prompt: Build: STOMP STOMP CLAP (rest)!

questions.hint: Body percussion uses only YOUR BODY!
questions.prompt: Which is NOT a body percussion sound?

questions.hint: Hitting and tapping!
questions.prompt: What does percussion mean?

options.text: Making sound by hitting, shaking, or scraping

questions.hint: Finger snaps are high and light!
questions.prompt: For a HIGH body percussion sound, which would you choose?

questions.hint: A steady beat keeps everyone together!
questions.prompt: Why is a steady beat important?

options.text: So the rhythm sounds organized and you can play with others

title: Concert Time!
description: It's showtime! Use everything you learned to put on a rhythm concert.
story_text: LADIES AND GENTLEMEN... welcome to the TinkerSchool Rhythm Concert! Chip is SO proud of you! Now it's time to put it ALL together for one amazing performance! Take a bow when you're done!

questions.hint: Stomp, clap, two quick snaps, dramatic pause!
questions.prompt: Build a 4-beat concert opening! STOMP, CLAP, quick-quick SNAP, REST!

questions.hint: Count-in, ABA form, big finish!

items.text: Finale: Big clap and take a bow!

questions.prompt: Build a grand finale! SOFTEST to LOUDEST for a crescendo!

items.text: Take a bow at the end!

questions.prompt: Put these concert rules in order!

questions.hint: Musical sandwich form!
questions.prompt: In our concert, Part A and Part B make the form ___.

questions.hint: Starts with C!
questions.prompt: Getting gradually louder is called a ___.

questions.hint: The top number tells you!
questions.prompt: A measure in 4/4 time has ___ beats.

questions.hint: Making sounds by hitting and tapping your body!
questions.prompt: Clapping, stomping, and snapping are all types of body ___.
2 quarter notes + 1 half note = ___ beats.

title: Pixel Power
description: Discover pixel art! Every image on a screen is made of tiny squares called pixels. Create a simple pixel art picture using rectangles on your M5Stick!
story_text: Chip zooms in on his own display. "WHOA! When I zoom in really close, the picture is made of tiny SQUARES!" he says. "Each square is called a PIXEL. A pixel is the tiniest piece of a picture. My screen has 135 pixels across and 240 pixels tall. That is... a LOT of tiny squares! If I color each pixel, I can make any picture I want. Let us start with something simple and zoom in to see the pixels!"

Lcd.drawString("Pixel Heart!", 20, 120)

hints.text: Pixels are tiny colored squares! When you put lots of them together, they make a picture. Lets build a pixel heart!
Each pixel is a small filled rectangle. Start with 20x20 pixel squares. Place one red square at (40, 20).
Add more red squares next to it and below. Follow a heart shape: wider in the middle, pointed at the bottom.
Leave two squares WHITE in the middle row for the heart highlight! That makes it look shiny.
Label your art with display text. You just made pixel art -- the same way video game characters are made!

story.title: The Tiny Squares
story.narrative: Chip discovers that his display is made of thousands of tiny squares. What are pixels?

create.title: My First Pixel Art
create.prompt: Color pixels on a small grid to make a simple picture. A heart? A star? A smiley face?

explore.title: Zoom In!

explore.device_interaction: Display shows image with zoom capability. Buttons zoom in/out to reveal pixel grid.

celebrate.title: Pixel Pioneer!
celebrate.summary: You discovered pixels! Every picture on every screen in the world is made of tiny colored squares.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child learned about digital image composition through pixels -- connecting technology to visual art.

title: Color Mixing
description: Mix primary colors to make NEW colors! Red + Blue = Purple, Red + Yellow = Orange, Blue + Yellow = Green. Show color mixing on your M5Stick!
story_text: Chip is experimenting with colors. "I found a secret," he whispers. "EVERY color on my screen is made from just THREE colors mixed together: Red, Green, and Blue! Red plus Green makes YELLOW! Red plus Blue makes PURPLE! And all three together make WHITE! It is like mixing paint, but with LIGHT. Use the buttons to turn each color up or down and watch what happens!"

hints.text: When you mix two primary colors, you get a SECONDARY color! Red + Blue = Purple. Lets show this on screen!
Draw a red circle, then a + sign, then a blue circle, then = sign, then a PURPLE circle. Thats the mix!
Do the same for Red + Yellow = Orange on the next row.
And Blue + Yellow = Green on the third row. Three mixing equations!
You just showed all three secondary colors! Purple, Orange, and Green are made from primary colors.

story_text: Chip is painting! But Chip only has red, blue, and yellow paint. What happens when you mix them? Let's find out!

Red + Blue = Purple. Yellow + Blue = Green. Show each mix with a result circle!

title: Pattern Maker
description: Artists love patterns! A pattern repeats shapes or colors in a design. Use loops to create a repeating pattern of shapes on your M5Stick screen!
story_text: Chip is looking at a quilt. "The same design keeps repeating over and over!" he says. "Red square, blue circle, red square, blue circle... That is a PATTERN! Patterns are everywhere in art -- on quilts, wallpaper, tiles, and wrapping paper. They are beautiful because they are predictable but also surprising. Let us make our own patterns! Tilt to shift the pattern around."
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="controls_repeat_ext"><value name="TIMES"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">5</field></shadow></value><statement name="DO"><block type="m5_display_circle"><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">30</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value><value name="RADIUS"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">red</field><field name="FILL">TRUE</field></block></statement></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
for i in range(5):
    x = 15 + i * 25
    Lcd.fillCircle(x, 40, 10, 0xFF0000)
    Lcd.fillRect(x - 8, 60, 16, 16, 0x0000FF)
for i in range(5):
    x = 15 + i * 25
    Lcd.fillCircle(x, 90, 10, 0xFF0000)
    Lcd.fillRect(x - 8, 110, 16, 16, 0x0000FF)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Pattern!", 30, 140)

hints.text: A pattern is a design that REPEATS! Circle-square-circle-square is a pattern. Lets make one with shapes!
Use a loop that repeats 5 times. Inside, draw a red filled circle. But we need each circle at a different X position!
After each circle, draw a blue square below it. Now you have a circle-square column that repeats!
Space them out: each one 25 pixels apart. The loop places them in a row automatically!
Add a second row below. Now you have a grid pattern of circles and squares -- art from code!

story.title: The Pattern Factory
story.narrative: Chip discovers patterns in quilts and tiles. Create your own beautiful repeating designs!

create.title: My Pattern Design

explore.title: Build a Pattern
explore.instructions: Choose a pattern unit (colors and shapes), then watch it repeat across the display grid. Tilt to shift!
explore.device_interaction: Display shows pattern grid. Buttons select elements. IMU tilt shifts pattern position.
explore.simulator_fallback: Click to design pattern units and see them tile across the screen.

celebrate.title: Pattern Artist!

celebrate.parent_note: Your child created visual repeating patterns, reinforcing mathematical pattern skills through art.

title: Symmetry Art
description: Symmetry means both sides look the same! Like a butterfly or a face. Create a symmetric design on your M5Stick where the left matches the right!
story_text: Chip folds a piece of paper on his screen. "Look! When I draw on one side and fold it, the other side is the SAME but flipped!" he says. "That is called SYMMETRY. Butterflies are symmetrical -- both wings match! Faces are symmetrical too. On my screen, you draw on the LEFT half and the RIGHT half copies it automatically. Like magic mirror art!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="m5_display_rect"><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">65</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value><value name="WIDTH"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">3</field></shadow></value><value name="HEIGHT"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">160</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">white</field><field name="FILL">TRUE</field></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.fillRect(66, 10, 2, 180, 0xFFFFFF)
Lcd.fillCircle(40, 50, 15, 0xFF0000)
Lcd.fillCircle(94, 50, 15, 0xFF0000)
Lcd.fillRect(25, 80, 30, 20, 0x0000FF)
Lcd.fillRect(79, 80, 30, 20, 0x0000FF)
Lcd.fillCircle(40, 130, 10, 0x00FF00)
Lcd.fillCircle(94, 130, 10, 0x00FF00)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Symmetry!", 25, 170)

hints.text: SYMMETRY means if you fold something in half, both sides match! Like a butterfly. Draw a line down the middle to start!
Draw a thin white line (rectangle) down the center: x=66, width=2, height=180. Thats the mirror line!
Now draw a red circle on the LEFT side at (40, 50). Then draw the SAME circle on the RIGHT at (94, 50). Mirror image!
Add blue rectangles: one on the left (25, 80) and one on the right (79, 80). Same size, same distance from center!
Add green circles too. Everything on the left has a twin on the right. Thats perfect symmetry!

story.title: Mirror Magic
story.narrative: Chip discovers symmetry -- when both halves match! Create mirror art on the display.

create.title: Symmetry Masterpiece
create.prompt: Create a symmetrical artwork! A butterfly? A robot face? A snowflake? Use the mirror to make it perfect!

explore.title: Draw and Mirror
explore.instructions: Draw on the left half of the display with buttons. The right half mirrors automatically! Tilt to change the axis of symmetry.

explore.simulator_fallback: Click to draw on one half and see the mirror image appear.

celebrate.title: Symmetry Artist!
celebrate.summary: You can see and create symmetry! Mirror images are one of nature's most beautiful patterns.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child explored symmetry through visual art, connecting to geometry and spatial reasoning.

title: Pixel Animals
description: Create a pixel art animal! Use small squares to build a cute cat, dog, or fish face. Pixel art is how video game characters are made!
story_text: Chip shows a tiny cat made of colored squares. "I drew this cat using only pixels!" he says proudly. "Pixel art is a special kind of art where you create pictures using a grid of colored squares. Old video games used pixel art because screens had fewer pixels. It is like a puzzle -- using just a few squares to make something everyone recognizes. Let us make pixel animals together!"

Add two smaller orange rectangles on top for triangular ears -- place them at the corners of the head.
Draw two small black circles for eyes. Place them evenly spaced on the face.
Add a pink circle for the nose and a thin black rectangle for the mouth. Add white whisker lines!
Label it with the cats name. You created pixel art -- just like game designers do!

story.title: The Pixel Zoo

title: Pattern Maker!
description: Design your own repeating pattern! Use shapes and colors to create a wallpaper-style pattern that tiles across the screen!
story_text: Chip is decorating a friendship bracelet with a pattern! Help put the shapes in the right order to keep the pattern going.
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="controls_repeat_ext"><value name="TIMES"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">3</field></shadow></value><statement name="DO"><block type="m5_display_rect"><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">30</field></shadow></value><value name="WIDTH"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value><value name="HEIGHT"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">red</field><field name="FILL">TRUE</field></block></statement></block></next></block></xml>

hints.text: A pattern repeats in rows and columns like a checkerboard or tile floor. Lets make one with alternating shapes!
Use two nested loops: one for rows, one for columns. The outer loop goes down, the inner loop goes across.
For even positions, draw a red square. For odd positions, draw a blue circle. Alternating!
Space them 27 pixels apart so they fill the screen nicely. 5 columns and 6 rows makes a full pattern!
Your pattern tiles like wallpaper! Real designers use this exact technique to create repeating designs!

questions.hint: The pattern alternates: circle, square, circle, square!
questions.prompt: Complete the pattern: circle, square, circle, square...

questions.hint: Think ROY G BIV -- Red, Orange, Yellow, Green, Blue!
questions.prompt: Put the rainbow colors in order!

title: Animation Basics
description: Make things move! Animation is pictures that change quickly. Program your M5Stick to move a circle across the screen -- your first animation!
story_text: Chip draws a ball at the bottom of his screen. Then he draws it a little higher. Then higher. "Now watch!" he says, flipping through the drawings fast. "The ball is BOUNCING! That is ANIMATION -- pictures shown really fast to create the illusion of movement. Movies show 24 pictures every SECOND! We will start with 2-3 frames. Each frame is slightly different, and when you flip through them -- MAGIC! Things move!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="m5_display_circle"><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">80</field></shadow></value><value name="RADIUS"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">15</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">yellow</field><field name="FILL">TRUE</field></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Animation!", 25, 10)
for i in range(12):
    Lcd.fillCircle(10 + i * 10, 80, 15, 0x000000)
    Lcd.fillCircle(20 + i * 10, 80, 15, 0xFFFF00)
    time.sleep(0.15)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("It moved!", 30, 120)

hints.text: Animation is an ILLUSION of movement! You draw something, erase it, draw it in a new spot. Do this fast and it looks like its moving!
Draw a yellow filled circle at position (20, 80). Thats frame 1 of your animation!
Now erase it (draw a black circle same spot) and draw it again 10 pixels to the right. Use a loop!
Keep the wait short (0.15 seconds) between frames. Faster = smoother animation!
Congratulations -- you just animated! Movies and games work the same way: lots of pictures shown fast!

story.title: Moving Pictures
story.narrative: Chip discovers that flipping drawings quickly creates the illusion of movement. Make your first animation!

create.title: My Animation
create.prompt: Create a 3-frame animation of anything you want. A jumping frog? A winking face? A growing flower?

explore.title: Frame by Frame
explore.instructions: Draw 2-3 frames of a simple animation. Press a button to flip through them rapidly and see movement!
explore.device_interaction: Display shows drawing canvas. Buttons switch frames and play animation. Draw slightly different pictures in each frame.
explore.simulator_fallback: Click to draw frames and play your animation.

celebrate.title: Animator!
celebrate.summary: You created an animation! Every cartoon and movie you watch uses this same trick -- pictures shown really fast.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child learned animation fundamentals (frame-by-frame) connecting art to technology and sequential thinking.

title: Color Moods
description: Colors can show feelings! Warm colors (red, orange, yellow) feel happy and energetic. Cool colors (blue, green, purple) feel calm. Show moods with colors!
story_text: Chip fills his screen with red and orange. "This feels like a warm campfire!" he says. Then he switches to blue and purple. "And this feels like a cool, calm ocean." Colors have FEELINGS! Warm colors (red, orange, yellow) feel energetic and happy. Cool colors (blue, green, purple) feel calm and peaceful. Artists choose colors to set the MOOD of their art. Let us explore how colors make you feel!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="m5_display_rect"><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">0</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">30</field></shadow></value><value name="WIDTH"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">135</field></shadow></value><value name="HEIGHT"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">40</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">red</field><field name="FILL">TRUE</field></block></next></block></xml>

hints.text: Artists choose colors to show FEELINGS! Red, orange, and yellow are WARM colors -- they feel happy and energetic!
Draw three warm color rectangles in a row: Red, Orange (use yellow), Yellow. They feel like sunshine!
Below that, draw COOL colors: Blue, Green, Purple. These feel calm and peaceful like the ocean.
Label the rows: WARM = Happy and COOL = Calm.
Next time you draw, think about what MOOD you want. Color choice is a powerful art tool!

story.title: Color Feelings
story.narrative: Chip discovers that colors create moods and feelings. Warm colors feel different from cool colors!

create.title: Mood Painting

explore.simulator_fallback: Click to explore color palettes with matching audio.

celebrate.title: Color Mood Expert!
celebrate.summary: You understand how colors create feelings! Artists use this knowledge in every painting and design.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child explored color theory (warm/cool) and its connection to emotional expression in art.

title: Art and Math
description: Art and math are connected! Use math to create geometric art -- draw shapes at calculated positions to make a beautiful symmetric design!
story_text: Chip connects the dots -- literally! "Art and math are best friends!" he says. "A pixel grid uses COORDINATES -- each pixel has a position like (3, 5). That means column 3, row 5! Shapes are geometry. Patterns use repetition. Symmetry uses mirror lines. Even mixing colors uses numbers (RGB values)! Let us use math to create art and art to see math!"

solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Art + Math!", 20, 5)
colors = [0xFF0000, 0xFF9800, 0xFFFF00, 0x00FF00, 0x0000FF, 0xFF00FF]
for i in range(6):
    r = 10 + i * 8
    Lcd.drawCircle(67, 100, r, colors[i])
for i in range(4):
    x = 20 + i * 30
    Lcd.fillRect(x, 30 + i * 5, 15, 15, colors[i])
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Math makes art!", 5, 180)

hints.text: Math and art work together! Circles inside circles, shapes in a row with spacing -- thats math creating beauty!
Draw circles at the same center (67, 100) but with INCREASING radius: 10, 18, 26, 34... Each is 8 bigger!
Use a different color for each circle. Loops with math make this easy -- the radius increases each time!
Add a row of squares at calculated positions. Each one 30 pixels apart. Math gives perfect spacing!
Math gives art PRECISION. Computers use math for ALL their drawings. You are an art programmer!

title: Elements of Art
description: The 5 elements of art are: line, shape, color, value (light/dark), and space. Create a piece that demonstrates all 5 elements on your M5Stick!
story_text: Every awesome drawing, painting, and sculpture is made from the same building blocks! Chip calls them the Elements of Art. Once you know them, you'll start seeing them EVERYWHERE. Ready to become an art detective?

hints.text: Every artwork uses 5 basic ELEMENTS: Line, Shape, Color, Value (light/dark), and Space (empty areas). Lets show all 5!
LINE: Draw a white horizontal line. SHAPE: Draw a filled circle and label it.
COLOR: Draw two rectangles in different colors side by side to show color variety.
VALUE: Draw a dark gray square next to a white square. Thats light vs dark (value)!
SPACE: Draw a circle outline with empty space inside. The space around art matters too! All 5 elements!

cards.back: A line is a mark that moves from one point to another. Lines can be straight, curved, zigzag, wavy, thick, or thin! Every drawing starts with a line.
A shape is a flat area with edges. Circles, squares, triangles, and stars are all shapes! When lines connect, they make shapes.
Color is what we see when light bounces off things. Colors have three parts: hue (the name, like red), value (light or dark), and intensity (bright or dull).
Texture is how something feels (or looks like it would feel). Art can look rough, smooth, bumpy, fuzzy, or prickly -- even on a flat piece of paper!
Space is the area around and inside things in art. Positive space is where the subject is. Negative space is the empty area around it.

activities.prompt: Flip each card to learn about the Elements of Art!

questions.hint: When you draw a line that goes around and meets itself, you get a circle, square, or triangle -- that's a shape!
questions.prompt: Which element of art is made when a line connects back to itself?

questions.hint: The fur looks soft and fluffy -- that's how it would FEEL if you touched it. That's texture!
questions.prompt: You draw a fluffy cat with soft-looking fur. Which element of art does the fur show?

questions.hint: These are all marks that move from one point to another -- just different types of the same element!
questions.prompt: A zigzag, a curve, and a straight mark are all examples of ___.

questions.hint: The empty area around and between objects in art is called negative space!
questions.prompt: The empty background around a drawing of a tree is called ___.

questions.hint: Count them: line, shape, color, texture, space!
questions.prompt: How many main elements of art did we learn about?

hints.text: Piet Mondrian was a famous artist who used only rectangles and the three primary colors plus black and white. Lets make art like him!
Start with a WHITE background (clear screen white). Then draw a big RED rectangle in one area.
Add a BLUE rectangle and a YELLOW rectangle in other areas. Leave some areas white!
Draw thick BLACK lines (rectangles) between the colored areas. Horizontal and vertical lines make a grid!
Your Mondrian-style art uses simple shapes and primary colors to create something beautiful and balanced!

cards.back: Monet loved painting outdoors! He painted the same garden pond with water lilies over and over in different light. He used soft, blurry brushstrokes to show how light changes. His style is called Impressionism.

Van Gogh painted with thick, swirly brushstrokes and bold colors. His most famous painting, The Starry Night, shows a magical night sky with swirling stars. He painted over 900 paintings in just 10 years!
Frida painted colorful self-portraits (pictures of herself!) that told stories about her life and her Mexican heritage. She often included animals, flowers, and bright colors from Mexican folk art.
Kusama is known as the Princess of Polka Dots! She covers everything -- rooms, sculptures, pumpkins -- with colorful dots. Her infinity rooms use mirrors so the dots seem to go on forever!
Mondrian painted with only straight lines and primary colors (red, blue, yellow) plus black, white, and gray. His art looks like a colorful grid! His style shows that simple shapes can be beautiful.

title: Touch and See: Texture
description: Learn about textures! Discover how things feel -- rough, smooth, bumpy, and soft -- and spot them in art.
story_text: Hey artist! Chip has a question for you -- have you ever petted a fluffy cat? Touched a bumpy pinecone? Felt smooth glass? Those feelings have a special art word: TEXTURE! Texture is how something feels when you touch it -- or how it LOOKS like it would feel. Let's explore textures together!

back.text: Rough things feel scratchy and uneven when you touch them! Think of sandpaper, tree bark, or a brick wall. Artists can make rough textures by using thick paint or scratchy lines.
Smooth things feel flat and silky with no bumps! Think of glass, a balloon, or a polished stone. Artists can make smooth textures by blending paint gently.
Bumpy things have little hills and valleys you can feel! Think of bubble wrap, a basketball, or a strawberry. Artists can make bumpy textures with dots and small circles.
Soft things feel gentle and squishy! Think of cotton balls, a stuffed animal, or a fluffy cloud. Artists can make soft textures with light, feathery brush strokes.

activities.prompt: Flip each card to learn about different textures!
Match each object to how it feels! What texture does it have?

questions.hint: Think about petting a cat versus touching sandpaper -- that FEELING is texture!
questions.prompt: What is TEXTURE in art?

options.text: How something feels or looks like it would feel

questions.hint: Think about which one feels flat and silky with no bumps at all!
questions.prompt: Which of these has a SMOOTH texture?

questions.hint: Artists are magicians! They can use brush strokes, dots, and lines to make flat paintings LOOK like they have texture.
questions.prompt: Can artists show texture in a painting even though a painting is flat?

options.text: Yes! Artists can make things LOOK rough, soft, or bumpy

questions.hint: Clouds look light, fluffy, and gentle -- that's a soft texture!
questions.prompt: If you wanted to draw a fluffy cloud, which texture would you show?

title: Pattern Parade
description: Discover repeating patterns in art! Learn AB, ABB, and ABC patterns and create your own.
story_text: Chip LOVES patterns! Look around you -- patterns are EVERYWHERE! Stripes on a shirt, tiles on a floor, spots on a ladybug. A pattern is something that repeats over and over. Today we are going to learn three types of patterns and become pattern experts!

back.text: An AB pattern has TWO things that take turns! Like: red-blue-red-blue-red-blue or circle-square-circle-square. Two things repeating forever!
An ABB pattern has ONE thing, then TWO of another! Like: clap-stomp-stomp-clap-stomp-stomp or star-heart-heart-star-heart-heart.
An ABC pattern has THREE different things repeating! Like: red-blue-green-red-blue-green or circle-square-triangle-circle-square-triangle.

activities.prompt: Flip each card to learn about different pattern types!

questions.hint: An AB pattern goes: Star, Heart, Star, Heart -- two things taking turns!
questions.prompt: Put this AB pattern in the right order!

questions.hint: ABB means one circle, then TWO squares, then repeat! Circle, Square, Square, Circle, Square, Square.
questions.prompt: Put this ABB pattern in the right order!

questions.hint: ABC has THREE different things: Red, Blue, Green -- then it starts over! Red, Blue, Green.
questions.prompt: Put this ABC pattern in the right order!

questions.hint: AB means two things take turns. After red always comes...
questions.prompt: In an AB pattern: red, blue, red, blue, red, ___

questions.hint: ABB means one clap, then TWO stomps. You need one more stomp to finish the set!
questions.prompt: In an ABB pattern: clap, stomp, stomp, clap, stomp, ___

questions.hint: ABC has three things: circle, triangle, star. After triangle comes...
questions.prompt: In an ABC pattern: circle, triangle, star, circle, triangle, ___

questions.hint: Patterns go again and again and again -- they keep doing the same thing!
questions.prompt: A pattern is something that ___ over and over.

questions.hint: A and B -- that's how many letters? Count them!
questions.prompt: An AB pattern has ___ things that take turns.

title: Symmetry Butterflies
description: Discover symmetry -- when both halves of something look the same! Create beautiful symmetrical designs.
story_text: Chip just caught a butterfly! Well, not a real one -- a picture of one! And Chip noticed something amazing: if you fold a butterfly in half, BOTH SIDES MATCH! That magic matching is called SYMMETRY. Let's find symmetry everywhere!

back.text: Symmetry means both sides match! If you fold something in half and the two sides look the same, it is symmetrical. The fold line is called the LINE OF SYMMETRY.
Butterflies are perfectly symmetrical! The left wing has the same colors and patterns as the right wing. Nature is a great artist!
The line of symmetry is like a mirror down the middle. Whatever is on one side appears on the other side too! Some shapes have one line of symmetry, and some have many.
Some things are NOT symmetrical -- and that's cool too! A hand waving, a tree blowing in the wind, or a paint splatter. These are called ASYMMETRICAL.

activities.prompt: Flip each card to learn about symmetry!
Match each thing to whether it is symmetrical or not!

questions.hint: Think about folding a butterfly in half -- what happens?
questions.prompt: What does SYMMETRY mean?

options.text: Both halves look the same when folded

questions.hint: Imagine folding paper in half -- that fold is the line of symmetry!
questions.prompt: What is the LINE OF SYMMETRY?

options.text: A line you draw around the outside
The fold line where both halves match
A line at the bottom of the drawing
The longest line in a picture

questions.hint: Try drawing a line down the middle of each letter. Which one has matching sides?
questions.prompt: Which letter of the alphabet is SYMMETRICAL?

questions.hint: Look at a butterfly's wings -- the left side mirrors the right side!

options.text: Its left and right wings have the same pattern

questions.hint: The prefix 'a' means 'not.' So 'a-symmetrical' means not symmetrical!

title: Patterns in Nature
description: Find amazing patterns in nature! Discover how flowers, shells, snowflakes, and more use patterns and textures.
story_text: Chip just came back from an adventure outside and WOW -- nature is the BEST artist! Flowers have patterns, seashells have spirals, snowflakes have symmetry, and leaves have amazing textures. Let's go on a nature pattern hunt together!

back.text: Flowers have radial patterns -- petals spread out from the center like a sun! Many flowers have 5 or 6 petals arranged in a perfect circle. Sunflowers even have spiral patterns in their seeds!
Seashells have spiral patterns that swirl around and around, getting bigger as they go! This spiral pattern is found in snail shells, nautilus shells, and even hurricanes!
Every snowflake has SIX arms, and each arm matches the others! Snowflakes are a beautiful example of symmetry in nature. No two snowflakes are exactly alike!
Leaves have wonderful textures! They have veins that branch out like tiny roads. Some leaves are smooth, some are fuzzy, some are waxy, and some have bumpy edges!

activities.prompt: Flip each card to discover patterns in nature!
Match each thing in nature to the pattern or texture it shows!

questions.hint: Snowflakes always have six arms -- and each arm matches the others!
questions.prompt: How many arms does every snowflake have?

questions.hint: Seashells swirl around and around, getting bigger -- that's a spiral!
questions.prompt: What kind of pattern does a seashell show?

questions.hint: Black stripe, white stripe, black stripe, white stripe -- that's a repeating pattern!
questions.prompt: A zebra's stripes are an example of what?

questions.hint: Look around! Nature has spirals in shells, symmetry in snowflakes, stripes on zebras, and so much more!
questions.prompt: Why is nature a great place to find art patterns?

options.text: Nature is full of spirals, symmetry, textures, and repeating patterns

questions.hint: Look closely at a leaf -- you will see tiny lines that branch out like roads. Those are veins!
questions.prompt: Which part of a leaf creates a branching pattern?

title: Design Your Own Pattern
description: Use everything you have learned about texture, patterns, and symmetry to design your own repeating pattern!
story_text: Chip is SO proud of you! You have learned about textures, patterns, and symmetry -- now it is time to put it ALL together! Let's test your pattern brain and see if you can complete patterns, fix sequences, and think like a real pattern designer!

questions.hint: AB means two shapes take turns. After circle always comes...

title: Color and Mood

story_text: Hey art explorer! Chip here! Did you know that colors aren't just pretty to look at -- they can actually change how you FEEL? Some colors make you feel excited and full of energy, while others make you feel calm and peaceful. Let's explore the amazing connection between colors and feelings!

To create a mood or feeling for the viewer

title: Happy Art vs Calm Art
description: Compare artwork that feels happy and joyful with artwork that feels calm and quiet. Learn to spot the differences!
story_text: Chip has been looking at SO much art lately! Some paintings make Chip want to dance around, and others make Chip feel like taking a cozy nap. Today we're going to learn how to tell if artwork feels HAPPY and bouncy or CALM and quiet. The secret is in the colors, shapes, and lines!

options.text: Bright neon colors and sharp zigzag lines
Only black and white with thick lines

Lots of tiny dots in random places

title: Meet Mondrian
description: Meet Piet Mondrian, the artist who made beautiful art with just straight lines, rectangles, and primary colors!
story_text: Chip found an artist who ONLY uses straight lines and a few colors -- and his art is AMAZING! His name is Piet Mondrian, and he proved that you don't need complicated drawings to make something beautiful. Just lines, rectangles, and primary colors. Let's explore his world of geometric art!

back.text: Piet Mondrian (1872-1944) was a Dutch artist who believed that the simplest shapes and colors could make the most powerful art. He moved from the Netherlands to Paris and later to New York City!
Mondrian's famous paintings are made of straight black lines that create rectangles. He filled some rectangles with red, blue, or yellow, and left others white. It looks like a colorful grid!

front.text: What does Mondrian's art look like?

back.text: Mondrian believed straight lines -- horizontal and vertical -- were the most pure and balanced. He never used diagonal or curvy lines in his famous grid paintings! He wanted perfect order and harmony.
Mondrian used only red, blue, and yellow (the primary colors) plus black, white, and gray. He thought these were the most basic, essential colors. Simple but powerful!
Mondrian's art feels organized, balanced, and calm. The straight lines and clean shapes create a feeling of order and harmony. Some people also think it looks fun -- like a colorful puzzle!

front.text: How does Mondrian's art make people feel?

activities.prompt: Flip each card to learn about Piet Mondrian and his art!
Match each fact about Mondrian to the correct answer!

questions.hint: Mondrian believed straight lines were the most pure -- no curves allowed!
questions.prompt: What kind of lines did Mondrian use in his famous grid paintings?

questions.hint: He used the three PRIMARY colors plus black and white -- just five colors total!
questions.prompt: Which colors did Mondrian use in his famous paintings?

options.text: All the colors of the rainbow
Red, blue, yellow, black, and white

questions.hint: When straight lines cross each other horizontally and vertically, they create this four-sided shape!
questions.prompt: What shape appears most in Mondrian's art?

questions.hint: Mondrian showed the whole world that SIMPLE can be AMAZING!
questions.prompt: What can we learn from Mondrian's art?

options.text: Art must be complicated to be beautiful
Simple shapes and colors can make powerful art
You need lots of different colors
Only famous artists can make good art

title: Meet Kandinsky
description: Meet Wassily Kandinsky, the artist who painted music! He used colorful circles, shapes, and wild lines to show how sounds and feelings look.
story_text: Chip just discovered something WILD! There was an artist named Wassily Kandinsky who said he could SEE music as colors and shapes! When he heard a trumpet, he saw bright yellow. When he heard a cello, he saw deep blue. He turned sounds and feelings into amazing abstract art. Let's explore his colorful world!

back.text: Wassily Kandinsky (1866-1944) was a Russian artist who is often called the father of abstract art. He was one of the first artists to paint pictures that didn't look like real things -- just shapes, colors, and lines!

Circles were Kandinsky's favorite shape! He painted many artworks full of colorful, overlapping circles of different sizes. He called the circle the most peaceful shape because it has no corners or sharp edges.

Kandinsky believed colors and shapes could be like music! He gave his paintings musical names like 'Composition' and 'Improvisation.' He said looking at a painting should feel like listening to a song.
Kandinsky gave every color a feeling! Yellow was exciting like a trumpet blast. Blue was deep and calm like a cello. Red was strong and confident. Green was peaceful and still.
Abstract art doesn't try to look like real things (like a house or a dog). Instead, it uses colors, shapes, and lines to express FEELINGS and IDEAS. Kandinsky helped invent this style!

activities.prompt: Flip each card to learn about Wassily Kandinsky!

questions.hint: He painted LOTS of these round shapes -- he called them the most peaceful shape!
questions.prompt: What was Kandinsky's favorite shape?

questions.hint: His art doesn't look like real things -- it uses shapes and colors to show feelings and ideas!
questions.prompt: Kandinsky is often called the father of what kind of art?

questions.hint: Remember Mondrian's straight lines and rectangles? Kandinsky was the opposite -- circles, curves, and wild lines!
questions.prompt: How is Kandinsky's art DIFFERENT from Mondrian's art?

options.text: Kandinsky used lots of curvy shapes; Mondrian used only straight lines
Mondrian used more colors than Kandinsky
Kandinsky only used black and white

questions.hint: When Kandinsky heard sounds, his brain turned them into colors and shapes -- that's why he 'painted music'!
questions.prompt: What special thing could Kandinsky do when he heard music?

options.text: He could see colors and shapes in his mind
He could make the music louder
He could write the notes down

questions.prompt: According to Kandinsky, yellow felt like which instrument?

questions.hint: Abstract art doesn't copy real things -- it expresses feelings through colors and shapes!

title: What Are Pixels?
description: Discover that every picture on a screen is made of tiny colored squares called pixels!
story_text: Hey there, art explorer! Chip here! Have you ever looked REALLY closely at a TV or tablet screen? If you zoom in super close, you'll find something amazing -- the picture is made of tiny little colored squares! These squares are called PIXELS, and they're the building blocks of every digital image. Let's learn all about them!

back.text: A pixel is a tiny colored square. It is the smallest piece of a digital image. The word pixel comes from "picture element" -- it is one little element of the whole picture!
Your tablet, phone, and TV screen are all made of millions of tiny pixels! They are so small you usually can not see them, but zoom in close and you will see little colored squares.
When lots of pixels are put together, each one a different color, they form a picture! It is like a mosaic or a quilt -- lots of tiny pieces make one big image.
Pixel art is a style of digital art where you can see each individual pixel. Artists carefully choose the color of every single square to make cool pictures, characters, and scenes!
Old video games from the 1980s and 1990s used pixel art because screens did not have many pixels. Today pixel art is still super popular because it looks fun and charming!

activities.prompt: Flip each card to learn about pixels!

questions.hint: Think about what you see if you zoom in REALLY close on a screen -- tiny colored squares!
questions.prompt: What is a pixel?

options.text: A tiny colored square that makes up digital images

questions.hint: A pixel is one little ELEMENT of a PICTURE -- put those words together!
questions.prompt: What does the word 'pixel' come from?

questions.hint: Think of a mosaic -- many small pieces come together to create the big picture!
questions.prompt: How do pixels make a picture?

options.text: One giant pixel shows the whole picture
Pixels only make sounds, not pictures
Many tiny colored pixels are placed together to form an image
Pixels can only be black and white

questions.hint: Old screens were not as powerful as today -- they could only show a small number of pixels!
questions.prompt: Why did old video games use pixel art?

options.text: Because screens did not have many pixels back then
Because players did not like colorful art
Because pixel art was illegal before 2000

questions.hint: Which one has a glowing screen? Screens are made of tiny pixels!

title: My First Pixel Art: Simple Shapes

story_text: Time to start creating! The coolest thing about pixel art is that you build pictures one square at a time. Today we're going to learn how to draw simple shapes on a grid -- like hearts, stars, and more! Even the most amazing pixel artists started with simple shapes. Let's go!

questions.hint: Pixel art is made of tiny colored shapes that have four equal sides!

questions.hint: Rows go across and ___ go up and down!
questions.prompt: A pixel grid is like graph paper with rows and ___.

questions.hint: Think about what color hearts usually are -- the color of love!
questions.prompt: To make a pixel art heart, you use ___ colored pixels in the shape of a heart.

questions.hint: You put the colored squares next to each other in a horizontal ___!
questions.prompt: To make a straight line in pixel art, you color pixels in a ___.

questions.hint: It looks like graph paper with squares -- it starts with the letter G!
questions.prompt: Pixel art uses a ___ to organize where each colored square goes.

right.text: Two bumps on top, point at bottom

Start with one pixel, add more each row

title: Pixel Art Animals

story_text: Chip loves animals, and pixel art animals are the CUTEST! Today you're going to learn how artists turn real animals into adorable pixel art. The trick is to keep it simple -- use just a few colors and focus on the most important features like eyes, ears, and a nose. Let's create some pixel pets!

title: Pixel Art with Coordinates
description: Use x and y coordinates to place pixels in the right spots! Pixel art meets math in this fun crossover lesson.
story_text: Guess what? Pixel art and MATH are best friends! Every pixel on a grid has an address, just like every house on a street. We use two numbers called coordinates to say exactly where a pixel goes. The first number (x) tells you how far RIGHT to go, and the second number (y) tells you how far DOWN to go. Let's use coordinates to make pixel art!

questions.hint: X goes left and right -- like reading a book, you start at the left and go ___!
questions.prompt: In a pixel grid, the x coordinate tells you how far ___ to go.

questions.hint: Y goes up and down! On a pixel grid, y usually counts downward from the top.
questions.prompt: The y coordinate tells you how far ___ to go on the grid.

questions.hint: The second number in the coordinate tells you how far down to go!
questions.prompt: The coordinate (3, 1) means go 3 squares right and ___ square(s) down.

questions.hint: It starts with the letter C -- it is the address of a pixel on the grid!
questions.prompt: Two numbers that give the position of a pixel are called ___.

questions.hint: Position (1, 1) means only 1 square right and 1 square down -- that is the corner where you start!
questions.prompt: The pixel at position (1, 1) is in the top-___ corner of the grid.

activities.prompt: Match each coordinate to where it is on the grid!

Now Chip wants YOU to plan your very own pixel art masterpiece! Real artists always plan before they create, so let's think about what you want to make and how you'll make it. This is YOUR chance to be a pixel art creator!

title: Spot the Pattern
description: Train your pattern-spotting brain! Display a number sequence on your M5Stick and reveal the hidden pattern rule!
story_text: Chip is staring at a row of shapes. "Red circle, blue square, red circle, blue square..." he mutters. "Wait -- I see it! It is a PATTERN! The same things keep repeating. Patterns are everywhere -- in music, in math, in nature, even in your daily routine! If you can spot the pattern, you can predict what comes NEXT. That is like a superpower for your brain. Let us train our pattern-spotting skills!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="m5_display_text"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">2, 4, 6, ...</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">50</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">white</field></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Spot the Pattern!", 2, 10)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("2, 4, 6, 8, ?", 10, 40)
time.sleep(2)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Rule: Add 2!", 15, 65)
Lcd.drawString("Answer: 10!", 20, 85)
Speaker.tone(523, 200)
time.sleep(1)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("5,10,15,20, ?", 5, 115)
time.sleep(2)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Rule: Add 5!", 15, 135)
Lcd.drawString("Answer: 25!", 20, 155)
Speaker.tone(523, 200)

hints.text: Number patterns have a RULE. In 2, 4, 6, 8 the rule is +2 each time. Lets show patterns and their rules!
Display the sequence: 2, 4, 6, 8, ? -- with a question mark for the missing number.
Wait a moment (let the viewer think!) then reveal the rule: Add 2 each time!
Show the answer: 10! Then try another pattern: 5, 10, 15, 20, ? (rule: add 5, answer: 25).
Play a success sound when each answer is revealed. Pattern spotting is a superpower for problem solving!

story.title: The Pattern Detective
story.narrative: Chip discovers that patterns are everywhere. Train your brain to spot and predict them!

create.title: My Pattern

explore.title: Find the Pattern

explore.device_interaction: Display shows pattern sequences. Buttons select the next element.
explore.simulator_fallback: Click to select what comes next in each pattern.

celebrate.title: Pattern Spotter!
celebrate.summary: You can spot patterns like a detective! This skill helps in math, music, reading, and science.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child practiced pattern recognition across multiple representations (visual, numeric, abstract).

title: What Comes Next?
description: Level up your pattern skills! Now with shapes -- circle, square, circle, square. Can you predict what comes next? Program the answer!
story_text: Chip is trying to make a sandwich. "I put the bread on top... wait, I need to put the peanut butter on first! And before THAT, I need to get the bread out. Things need to happen in the right ORDER!" he says. "First, get the bread. Next, spread peanut butter. Then, put the top on. FIRST, NEXT, LAST -- that is SEQUENCING! Let us practice putting things in order."
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="m5_display_circle"><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">20</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">60</field></shadow></value><value name="RADIUS"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">yellow</field><field name="FILL">TRUE</field></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Shape Pattern!", 10, 10)
for i in range(4):
    x = 15 + i * 30
    if i % 2 == 0:
        Lcd.fillCircle(x, 55, 10, 0xFFFF00)
    else:
        Lcd.fillRect(x - 10, 45, 20, 20, 0x00FF00)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("O [] O [] ?", 10, 80)
time.sleep(2)
Lcd.fillCircle(15 + 4 * 30, 55, 10, 0xFFFF00)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("CIRCLE!", 35, 105)
Speaker.tone(523, 300)

hints.text: Shape patterns alternate between different shapes! Circle-Square-Circle-Square... whats next?
Draw a yellow circle, then a green square, then circle, then square. Alternate in a row!
Display the pattern using letters: O [] O [] ? (O for circle, [] for square).
Wait for a moment, then draw the answer: a CIRCLE! The pattern starts with circle.
Patterns can use shapes, colors, numbers, or anything! The key is finding what REPEATS.

story.title: Order Matters!
story.narrative: Chip tries to make a sandwich but does the steps in the wrong order. Learn about sequencing!

create.title: My Sequence
create.prompt: Write the steps for something you do every day (like getting ready for bed). What order do things go in?

explore.title: Sequence Sorter
explore.instructions: Display shows events out of order. Tilt to rearrange them. Press button when they are in the correct sequence!
explore.device_interaction: Display shows scrambled events. IMU tilt rearranges. Buttons confirm order.
explore.simulator_fallback: Drag events into the correct sequence.

celebrate.title: Sequence Star!
celebrate.summary: You can put events in order! Sequencing helps you plan, follow instructions, and tell stories.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child practiced sequential ordering and identifying logical step progressions.

title: Sort It Out
description: Sorting means putting things in order! Sort numbers from smallest to biggest, or colors from light to dark. Display a sorting challenge!
story_text: Chip has a messy pile of things. "I have apples, bananas, a toy car, a doll, grapes, and a teddy bear," he says. "How do I organize them? I could sort them into FRUITS and TOYS! Apples, bananas, grapes go in one group. Car, doll, teddy bear in another. SORTING means finding what things have in common and grouping them together. Tilt LEFT to put things in one bucket, RIGHT for the other!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="m5_display_text"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">Sort These!</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">15</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">yellow</field></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Sort These!", 20, 10)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFF0000, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("9  3  7  1  5", 15, 35)
time.sleep(2)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Sorted:", 35, 60)
nums = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9]
for i in range(5):
    Lcd.fillRect(10 + i * 25, 120 - nums[i] * 8, 20, nums[i] * 8, 0x0000FF)
    Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(nums[i]), 15 + i * 25, 125)
    Speaker.tone(300 + nums[i] * 30, 150)
    time.sleep(0.3)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("1 3 5 7 9!", 20, 145)

hints.text: SORTING means putting things in order. Smallest to biggest, A to Z, lightest to darkest. Lets sort numbers!
Show the unsorted numbers in red: 9, 3, 7, 1, 5. They are all mixed up!
Wait a moment, then show them sorted: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9. Smallest to biggest!
Draw bars of different heights to SHOW the sorting -- tall bar for big numbers, short for small.
Play tones that go UP as numbers get bigger. You can HEAR that its sorted! Sorting is a fundamental skill!

story.title: The Big Sort
story.narrative: Chip has a messy pile of things. Learn to classify objects by finding common attributes!

create.title: My Sorting Game
create.prompt: Find 10 objects near you and sort them into 2 groups. What is your sorting rule? Can someone guess it?

explore.title: Tilt to Sort
explore.instructions: Display shows items one at a time. Tilt left for Group A, right for Group B. Figure out the sorting rule!
explore.device_interaction: Display shows items. IMU tilt sorts into left/right buckets. Buzzer confirms correct sort.
explore.simulator_fallback: Drag items into the correct group.

celebrate.title: Sorting Star!
celebrate.summary: You can classify and sort like a scientist! Grouping things helps us understand the world.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child practiced classification by single and multiple attributes, a key logical thinking skill.

title: What Comes Next?
description: Advanced patterns! The pattern gets trickier with growing sequences. Display a pattern that changes each time and predict the next element!
story_text: Chip loves puzzles! Look at each pattern carefully and figure out what comes next. You are a pattern detective!
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="m5_display_text"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">Growing!</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">25</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">yellow</field></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Growing Pattern!", 2, 5)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("1, 3, 5, 7, ?", 15, 30)
time.sleep(2)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("+2 each time!", 10, 50)
Lcd.drawString("Answer: 9!", 25, 68)
Speaker.tone(440, 200)
time.sleep(1)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("1, 2, 4, 8, ?", 15, 95)
time.sleep(2)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("x2 each time!", 10, 115)
Lcd.drawString("Answer: 16!", 20, 133)
Speaker.tone(523, 300)

hints.text: Growing patterns get bigger each time! But HOW they grow is the key. Some add, some multiply!
Pattern 1: 1, 3, 5, 7, ? -- each number adds 2. So next is 9!
Show the rule: +2 each time. Then reveal the answer with a sound.
Pattern 2: 1, 2, 4, 8, ? -- each number DOUBLES! So next is 16!
Two different growing patterns: one adds, one multiplies. Finding the rule is the puzzle!

questions.hint: The pattern goes apple, banana, apple, banana... what comes after apple?
questions.prompt: Pattern: 🍎 🍌 🍎 🍌 🍎 ___

questions.hint: Two reds, then a blue: red, red, BLUE, red, red, ___
questions.prompt: Pattern: 🔴 🔴 🔵 🔴 🔴 ___

questions.hint: The numbers repeat: 1, 2, 3, then start over: 1, 2, 3!
Two stars, then a moon. Two stars, then a moon. Two stars, then...

title: Cause and Effect
description: Every action has a reaction! Press button A and something happens. Press button B and something different happens. Explore cause and effect!
story_text: Chip pushes a ball. It rolls across the screen and knocks over blocks. "I PUSHED the ball -- that is the CAUSE. The blocks fell down -- that is the EFFECT!" he says. "Everything that happens has a reason. If I press a button, something happens on screen. If it rains, the ground gets wet. Cause and effect! Let us explore what happens when we do different things."
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_text" x="20" y="20"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">Press buttons!</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">30</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">yellow</field><next><block type="controls_if"><value name="IF0"><block type="m5_button_pressed"><field name="BUTTON">A</field></block></value></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Cause & Effect!", 5, 10)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("A=Sound B=Light", 5, 30)
for i in range(30):
    if BtnA.isPressed():
        Lcd.fillRect(0, 55, 135, 50, 0x000000)
        Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
        Lcd.drawString("CAUSE: Btn A", 10, 60)
        Lcd.drawString("EFFECT: Beep!", 10, 80)
        Speaker.tone(523, 200)
    if BtnB.isPressed():
        Lcd.fillRect(0, 55, 135, 50, 0x000000)
        Lcd.setTextColor(0x0000FF, 0x000000)
        Lcd.drawString("CAUSE: Btn B", 10, 60)
        Lcd.drawString("EFFECT: LED!", 10, 80)
        Power.setLed(255)
        time.sleep(0.3)
        Power.setLed(0)
    time.sleep(0.1)

hints.text: CAUSE and EFFECT: when you do something (cause), something happens (effect). Like pressing a light switch turns on a light!
Check IF button A is pressed. Thats the CAUSE. Inside the if block, play a tone. Thats the EFFECT!
Add another check for button B. Different CAUSE, different EFFECT -- flash the LED!
Display the cause and effect on screen so you can read what happened.
Put everything in a loop so the program keeps checking. Every button press (cause) makes something happen (effect)!

story.title: Chain Reaction
story.narrative: Chip discovers that every action has a reaction. Learn about cause and effect!

create.title: My Chain Reaction
create.prompt: Design a chain reaction: Event A causes Event B, which causes Event C. Like dominoes of events!

explore.title: If This, Then That
explore.instructions: Press buttons to trigger different causes. Watch the effects happen on the display! The buzzer confirms correct matches.
explore.device_interaction: Display shows scenarios. Buttons trigger causes. Screen shows effects. Buzzer confirms correct matching.
explore.simulator_fallback: Click causes and see their effects play out on screen.

celebrate.title: Cause & Effect Detective!
celebrate.summary: You understand that actions have consequences! This helps you predict what will happen next in any situation.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child practiced causal reasoning, a key critical thinking skill across all academic subjects.

title: Break It Down
description: Big problems become easy when you break them into small steps! Display a big task and then show it broken into 3 simple steps on your M5Stick!
story_text: Chip looks at a big, complicated task on his display. "Clean the WHOLE room?! That is too much!" he says. But then he thinks. "Wait... what if I break it into TINY steps? Step 1: pick up toys. Step 2: make the bed. Step 3: put books on the shelf. Now each step is EASY! Breaking a big problem into small pieces is called DECOMPOSITION. It makes anything possible!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="m5_display_text"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">Big Problem!</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">red</field></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFF0000, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("BIG Problem:", 10, 10)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Clean your room!", 5, 30)
time.sleep(1.5)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Break it down:", 5, 55)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
time.sleep(0.5)
Lcd.drawString("1. Pick up toys", 5, 75)
Speaker.tone(350, 150)
time.sleep(0.8)
Lcd.drawString("2. Make the bed", 5, 93)

time.sleep(0.8)
Lcd.drawString("3. Put away books", 5, 111)
Speaker.tone(450, 150)
time.sleep(0.8)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Easy now!", 30, 135)
Speaker.tone(523, 300)

hints.text: DECOMPOSITION means breaking a BIG problem into SMALL parts. Clean your room sounds big, but its really just a few small jobs!
Display the big scary problem first in RED: "Clean your room!" That seems hard!
Then break it into steps: 1. Pick up toys, 2. Make the bed, 3. Put away books. Small steps!
Show each step one at a time with a sound. Each small step is easy!
End with "Easy now!" -- breaking big problems into small steps makes ANYTHING solvable. Programmers do this every day!

story.title: The Big Problem Shrinker
story.narrative: Chip faces a huge task but learns to break it into tiny, manageable steps!

create.title: My Step-by-Step Plan
create.prompt: Pick something big you want to do (build a fort, learn a song). Break it into small steps. How many steps does it take?

explore.title: Step by Step
explore.instructions: Display shows a big task. Press buttons to reveal how it breaks into small steps. Then try breaking tasks down yourself!
explore.device_interaction: Display shows big task. Buttons reveal step-by-step breakdown one at a time.
explore.simulator_fallback: Click to reveal steps and try decomposing tasks yourself.

celebrate.title: Decomposition Pro!
celebrate.summary: You can break any big problem into small steps! This is how engineers and scientists solve the hardest problems.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child practiced decomposition, a core computational thinking skill used in programming and problem solving.

title: Maze Runner
description: Navigate a maze by giving directions! Program your M5Stick to show a path through a simple maze using step-by-step directions!
story_text: Chip is at the entrance of a maze on his display. "I need to get to the exit, but there are walls everywhere!" he says. "I cannot just rush in -- I need to PLAN AHEAD. Which way should I go? Left? Right? What if I hit a dead end? The trick is to look ahead and think about where each path leads BEFORE I start moving. Tilt me to navigate through the maze!"
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="m5_display_text"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">Maze!</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">40</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">5</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">yellow</field></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Maze Runner!", 15, 5)
Lcd.drawRect(10, 25, 115, 120, 0xFFFFFF)
Lcd.fillRect(25, 45, 80, 5, 0xFFFFFF)
Lcd.fillRect(25, 70, 5, 50, 0xFFFFFF)
Lcd.fillRect(50, 95, 60, 5, 0xFFFFFF)
Lcd.fillCircle(18, 35, 5, 0x00FF00)
steps = [(18,35),(18,55),(40,55),(40,80),(60,80),(60,110),(100,110),(100,130)]
for i in range(len(steps) - 1):
    x, y = steps[i]
    Lcd.fillCircle(x, y, 5, 0x000000)
    x2, y2 = steps[i + 1]
    Lcd.fillCircle(x2, y2, 5, 0x00FF00)
    Speaker.tone(400, 50)
    time.sleep(0.4)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Escaped!", 30, 155)

hints.text: A maze needs step-by-step directions to solve! Down, right, down, right -- each step moves you closer to the exit.
Draw the maze walls using white rectangles. Leave gaps for the path!
Place a green dot at the start. Then move it step by step through the maze.
Erase the old position (draw black) and draw the dot at the new position. Its like animation!
When the dot reaches the exit, show "Escaped!" Solving mazes uses planning and logical thinking!

story.title: The Amazing Maze
story.narrative: Chip faces a maze! Learn to plan ahead and think spatially to find the exit.

create.title: My Maze
create.prompt: Design your own maze! Then challenge a friend or family member to solve it. Make it tricky but possible!

explore.title: Tilt Through the Maze
explore.instructions: Tilt the device to move a dot through the maze. The buzzer sounds on wall hits. LED flashes on reaching the exit!
explore.device_interaction: Display shows maze. IMU tilt controls dot movement. Buzzer on wall collision. LED on success.
explore.simulator_fallback: Use arrow keys or click to navigate the maze.

celebrate.title: Maze Master!
celebrate.summary: You can navigate mazes by planning ahead! Spatial reasoning helps you think about space and direction.
celebrate.parent_note: Your child practiced spatial reasoning and planning through maze navigation, building executive function skills.

title: Sort It Out!
description: Sort numbers from smallest to biggest! Display jumbled numbers and show them being sorted into the right order!
story_text: Chip is cleaning up the toy room! Help sort everything into the right groups. Does it go with the animals or the vehicles?
starter_blocks_xml: <xml xmlns="https://developers.google.com/blockly/xml"><block type="m5_display_clear" x="20" y="20"><field name="COLOR">black</field><next><block type="m5_display_text"><value name="TEXT"><shadow type="text"><field name="TEXT">Sort!</field></shadow></value><value name="X"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">45</field></shadow></value><value name="Y"><shadow type="math_number"><field name="NUM">10</field></shadow></value><field name="COLOR">yellow</field></block></next></block></xml>
solution_code:
Lcd.fillScreen(0x000000)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Sort It Out!", 15, 5)
Lcd.setTextColor(0xFF0000, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("8  2  6  4", 20, 30)
time.sleep(1.5)
Lcd.setTextColor(0x00FF00, 0x000000)
Lcd.drawString("Smallest first:", 5, 55)
sorted_nums = [2, 4, 6, 8]
for i in range(4):
    h = sorted_nums[i] * 10
    Lcd.fillRect(15 + i * 30, 130 - h, 25, h, 0x0000FF)
    Lcd.setTextColor(0xFFFFFF, 0x000000)
    Lcd.drawString(str(sorted_nums[i]), 22 + i * 30, 135)
