title: My Score Counter
description: Use a variable to count button presses! Every time you press the button, the score goes up by one.
story_text: Hey there, coder! Chip here! Have you ever played a game where you keep score? Like counting how many baskets you make or how many jumping jacks you do? Well, today we're going to learn about something SUPER important in coding called a VARIABLE. A variable is like a special box with a name on it -- you can put a number inside, take it out, or change it whenever you want! We're going to make a score counter that goes up every time you press a button. Let's go!

questions.hint: Think of a box with a label on it -- you can put things inside and take them out!
questions.prompt: What is a variable in coding?

options.text: A named box that stores information

questions.prompt: If a variable called 'score' starts at 0, and you add 1 three times, what number is in the box now?

questions.hint: If you had 10 boxes, how would you know which one has the score? By reading the name on the box!
questions.prompt: Why do we give variables a NAME like 'score' instead of just saying 'the number'?

options.text: Because names make the code easier to understand

questions.hint: Think of something you put things inside -- it has a label on it!
questions.prompt: A variable is like a ___ that holds information.

questions.hint: 5 plus 1 equals...
questions.prompt: If score = 5 and we add 1, the new score is ___.

questions.hint: Before anyone scores any points, the score is...
questions.prompt: When we first create a score counter, we usually start it at ___.

title: Name Tag Maker
description: Store your name in a variable and display it on screen! Learn that variables can hold words, not just numbers.
story_text: Guess what, coder? Variables aren't just for numbers! They can hold WORDS too! Think about a name tag -- you know, the sticker that says "Hello, my name is..." on it? Well, today we're going to make a digital name tag! We'll store your name inside a variable and then display it on the M5Stick screen. The cool part is, you can change the name in the variable and the screen updates automatically. It's like magic!

questions.hint: Think about what you would store in a variable called 'name' -- it wouldn't be a number!
questions.prompt: Variables can store numbers AND ___.

questions.hint: 'Cassidy' is not a number -- it's a...
questions.prompt: A variable called 'name' that stores 'Cassidy' is holding a ___.

questions.hint: The screen always shows whatever is stored in the variable right now!
questions.prompt: If we change the 'name' variable from 'Cassidy' to 'Chip', the screen will show ___.

activities.prompt: Match each variable name to what it would store!

questions.hint: A good variable name tells you exactly what's inside -- like a label on a box!
questions.prompt: Which of these is the BEST name for a variable that stores someone's favorite food?

questions.hint: Imagine erasing a name on a whiteboard and writing a new one. The old name is gone!
questions.prompt: What happens when you change the value inside a variable?

options.text: The old value stays and you get two values
The old value is replaced by the new value

title: Random Surprise
description: Use a random number stored in a variable to pick a surprise display and sound! Every press is different!
story_text: Chip LOVES surprises! What if every time you pressed a button, something DIFFERENT happened? Maybe a star appears, or a heart, or a smiley face -- and you never know which one you'll get! That's what RANDOM numbers do in coding. The computer picks a number it can't even predict, and we store it in a variable. Then we use that number to choose what shows on screen and what sound plays. Let's build a surprise machine!

questions.hint: Random is like rolling a dice -- you never know what number you'll get!
questions.prompt: What does 'random' mean in coding?

options.text: The computer always picks the same number
The computer picks a number you can't predict
The computer picks the biggest number

questions.hint: We need a named box to hold the random number so we can check what it is!
questions.prompt: If a random number picks 1, 2, or 3, and each number shows a different emoji, what stores the picked number?

questions.hint: Random is like flipping a coin -- you can get heads twice in a row!
questions.prompt: You press the button and the random variable picks 2. You press again and it picks 2 again. Is that possible?

options.text: Yes! Random means any number can come up each time
No, it always goes in order: 1, 2, 3
Only if the battery is low

questions.hint: We need to save the number in our box so we can look at it and decide: show a star? A heart? A smiley?
questions.prompt: Why do we store the random number in a variable before using it?

options.text: So we can check the number and decide what to do

questions.hint: First you wait, then pick a number, save it, look at it, and finally show the result!

items.text: Show the matching emoji and play a sound

questions.prompt: Put these steps in order to make a random surprise happen when you press a button!

questions.hint: First create the box, then put a random number in it, then show what's inside!

items.text: Set 'surprise' to a random number (1, 2, or 3)
Display the value of 'surprise' on screen

title: Timer Countdown
description: Build a countdown timer that uses a variable! Watch the number go from 10 down to 0 with a beep each second.
story_text: Ready for liftoff? Chip loves countdowns! You know, like when a rocket launches: 10... 9... 8... 7... all the way to ZERO and then BLAST OFF! We're going to build our own countdown timer using a variable. The variable starts at 10, and every second it goes DOWN by 1. Plus, the buzzer beeps each time the number changes! This is a great way to see how a variable can CHANGE over time. Let's count down together!

questions.hint: 10, 9, 8, 7... the numbers are getting smaller. They are going...
questions.prompt: In a countdown timer, the variable starts at 10 and goes ___ by 1 each second.

questions.hint: 7 minus 1 equals...
questions.prompt: If the timer variable is 7, after subtracting 1 it becomes ___.

questions.hint: After 1 comes the very last number in a countdown... blast off!
questions.prompt: The countdown stops when the timer variable reaches ___.

questions.hint: Adding makes numbers bigger, subtracting makes them smaller. They go in different directions!
questions.prompt: Subtracting 1 from a variable is the ___ of adding 1.

questions.hint: Subtract means take away. If you have 10 and subtract 1, you get 9!
questions.prompt: What does it mean to 'subtract 1' from a variable?

questions.hint: Start at 10 and count down 4 times: 10, 9, 8, 7, ...
questions.prompt: The timer starts at 10. After 4 seconds of counting down (subtracting 1 each second), what number does the variable show?

questions.hint: Sound helps you know something is happening even if you're not looking at the screen!
questions.prompt: Why does the buzzer beep each second during the countdown?

options.text: To give audio feedback so you can hear the countdown
To make the timer go faster

questions.hint: The number in the variable goes from 10 all the way down to 0 -- it keeps changing!
questions.prompt: A variable that changes from 10 to 0 is an example of a variable that...

title: High Score Tracker
description: Track and compare scores using TWO variables! Learn to compare numbers and update the best score.
story_text: You know what makes games EXTRA fun? Trying to beat your best score! Chip once got 15 points in a game, and then tried SO hard to get 16. Today we're leveling up -- we're going to use TWO variables at the same time! One variable tracks your CURRENT score, and another variable remembers your BEST score ever. When your current score is higher than your best, the best score updates. This is how real video games work! Pretty cool, right?

questions.hint: You need one box for the score right now and another box to remember the best score ever!
questions.prompt: To track a high score, how many variables do we need?

options.text: Two -- current score AND best score

questions.hint: Is 8 bigger than 5? If current score beats the best score, we have a new best score!
questions.prompt: Current score is 8 and best score is 5. What should happen?

options.text: Best score updates to 8 because 8 is bigger than 5

questions.hint: Is 3 bigger than 10? Nope! So the best score doesn't change -- it keeps the bigger number.
questions.prompt: Current score is 3 and best score is 10. What should happen?

options.text: Best score stays at 10 because 3 is not higher

questions.hint: Comparing means looking at two things and figuring out how they relate -- is one bigger?
questions.prompt: What does 'comparing' two variables mean?

options.text: Checking which one is bigger, smaller, or equal

questions.hint: 12 is bigger than 9, so the best score updates to match the current score!
questions.prompt: If current_score is 12 and best_score is 9, the new best_score becomes ___.

questions.hint: One for current, one for best -- how many is that?
questions.prompt: We use ___ variables to track both the current score and the best score.

questions.hint: The best score is the BIGGEST score you've ever gotten. A new score has to be even ___!
questions.prompt: The best score only changes when the current score is ___ than the best score.

questions.hint: First get the score, then compare it, update if needed, and finally show both numbers!

items.text: Player finishes the round and gets a current score
Compare current score to the best score
If current score is higher, update best score
Display both scores on the screen

questions.prompt: Put these steps in order to check and update a high score!

questions.hint: Start at 0, then 5 beats 0 so it updates. Then 3 doesn't beat 5, so it stays!

items.text: Set best_score to 0 at the start
Play the game and get 5 points
Best score stays at 5 (3 is not > 5)

questions.prompt: Put these events in the right order for a game with high score tracking!

title: Digital Pet
description: Create a simple pet with needs like feeding and playing! Watch its mood change based on how you care for it.
story_text: Chip is SO excited for this one! We're going to build a DIGITAL PET that lives inside your device! Your pet will have feelings -- it can be happy, hungry, or bored. You'll use buttons to feed it and play with it, and its face will change to show how it feels. It's like having a tiny friend in your pocket! Let's bring our pet to life!

questions.hint: When you eat food, you stop being hungry and feel better! Your pet works the same way.
questions.prompt: Your digital pet is hungry! What should happen when you press the FEED button?

options.text: The pet's hunger goes down and it shows a happy face
The pet disappears from the screen

questions.hint: A variable is like a labeled box -- it holds a value that can change. We use one to track the pet's mood!
questions.prompt: What is a VARIABLE in our pet program?

options.text: A box that stores information, like how hungry the pet is

questions.hint: If nobody feeds or plays with a pet for a long time, it gets sad. Our program checks how long it's been!
questions.prompt: Your pet has been waiting a long time without food or play. What mood should it show?

options.text: Super happy with a big smile
Sad or upset because it needs attention
It doesn't matter -- moods never change

questions.hint: We need to CHECK something and then DO something based on the answer. That's an IF statement!
questions.prompt: Which coding concept helps the pet check IF it is hungry?

options.text: A conditional -- IF hungry THEN show sad face
A variable -- it stores a number
A display -- it shows text on screen

activities.prompt: Match each pet mood to what the screen should display!

items.text: Wait for the player to press Button A (Feed)
If yes, reduce the hunger number
Change the pet's face to happy
Display the new face on screen

title: Reaction Timer
description: Measure how fast you can press a button after seeing "GO!" on the screen. Can you beat your best time?
story_text: Hey speedster! Chip has a challenge for you -- how FAST are your reflexes? We're going to build a reaction timer game! The screen will say "WAIT..." and then suddenly flash "GO!" -- and you have to press the button as FAST as you can! The program will measure exactly how long it took you in milliseconds. That's thousandths of a second! Ready to test your speed?

questions.hint: The player needs to wait before they can react. First we show them to get ready!
questions.prompt: In our reaction timer, what should the screen show FIRST?

questions.hint: If GO always appeared after the same time, you could just count and press early. Random keeps it fair!
questions.prompt: Why does the program wait a RANDOM amount of time before showing GO?

options.text: So the player can't just guess when to press
It doesn't matter when GO appears

questions.hint: Reaction time is how long BETWEEN the two events. Subtracting gives us the difference!
questions.prompt: A variable stores the time when GO appeared, and another stores when the button was pressed. How do we find the reaction time?

options.text: Subtract: button time minus GO time

questions.hint: Pressing before GO is cheating! A good program catches this and gives a friendly do-over.
questions.prompt: What should happen if someone presses the button BEFORE 'GO!' appears?

options.text: Count it as a super fast time
Show 'Too early! Try again' and restart

questions.hint: Milli means one-thousandth. A second has 1000 of these!
questions.template: The reaction timer measures time in ___, which are thousandths of a second.

questions.hint: This type of number is unpredictable -- you never know what it will be!
questions.template: To make the wait time different each round, we use a ___ number.

questions.hint: These are like labeled boxes that hold values in our program!
questions.template: We store the start time and end time in ___ so we can subtract them later.

items.text: Wait a random amount of time
Display GO! and save the start time
Wait for the player to press the button
Calculate reaction time and show the result

activities.prompt: Put the reaction timer steps in the correct order!

title: Secret Message
description: Encode and decode secret messages using a simple code! Press a button to reveal the hidden message.
story_text: Psssst! Chip has a SECRET to tell you! Did you know that people have been sending hidden messages for THOUSANDS of years? Spies, kings, and even kids have used secret codes! Today we're going to build a program that can ENCODE a message (turn it into a secret code) and DECODE it (turn it back to normal). Press Button A to see the secret, press Button B to hide it again!

activities.prompt: In our simple code, each letter shifts forward by 1! Match the coded letter to the real letter.

questions.hint: This word starts with E and means putting a message INTO code!
questions.template: Turning a normal message into a secret code is called ___.

questions.hint: This word starts with D and means taking a message OUT of code!
questions.template: Turning a secret code back into a normal message is called ___.

questions.hint: H becomes I (one letter forward) and I becomes J (one letter forward)!
questions.template: If each letter shifts forward by 1, the coded word for HI is ___.

questions.hint: It's like a box that remembers which state the message is in right now!
questions.template: We use a ___ to store whether the message is showing the coded or decoded version.

questions.hint: If Button A reveals the secret, Button B should hide it again!
questions.prompt: In our program, Button A decodes the secret message. What should Button B do?

options.text: Encode the message back to the secret version

questions.hint: Check each letter: H+1=I, E+1=F, L+1=M, L+1=M, O+1=P. Does IFMMP match?
questions.prompt: Your coded message shows 'IFMMP' but should say 'HELLO'. Each letter shifted forward by 1. Is the code working correctly?

options.text: Yes! H→I, E→F, L→M, L→M, O→P -- that's correct!

questions.hint: If you encode a message and then decode it, you should get the SAME message back. Testing checks this!
questions.prompt: Why is it important to TEST your secret message program?

options.text: Testing is not important for simple programs
To make sure encoding and decoding give back the original message
Only professional programmers need to test

title: Simon Says
description: Remember and repeat a sequence of beeps and lights! The pattern gets longer each round -- how far can you go?
story_text: Time for a memory challenge! Chip LOVES this game! Simon Says is a classic -- the device plays a pattern of beeps and lights, and YOU have to repeat it back using the buttons. Each round, the pattern gets ONE note longer! It starts easy: beep! But soon it's beep-boop-beep-boop-beep! Your program needs to remember the whole sequence and check if the player gets it right. Let's build it!

items.text: The program creates a random sequence of tones
The device plays the tone sequence for the player
The player repeats the sequence using buttons
The program checks if the player got it right
If correct, add one more tone and repeat!

activities.prompt: Put the Simon Says game steps in the correct order!

questions.hint: We need to store a SEQUENCE of tones -- that means a list that keeps growing!
questions.prompt: In Simon Says, the pattern gets longer each round. What coding concept stores the growing list of tones?

options.text: A single variable that holds one number
A list (or array) that can hold many items

questions.hint: In Simon Says, the ORDER matters! The same notes in a different order is still wrong.
questions.prompt: The player presses: beep, boop, beep. But the correct pattern was: beep, beep, boop. What happened?

options.text: The player made a mistake -- the ORDER was wrong
It doesn't matter what order you press

questions.hint: Good games are kind when you lose! Show the score and let the player try again.
questions.prompt: What should the program do when the player makes a mistake?

options.text: Show the score, play a sad sound, and offer to try again

questions.hint: The game repeats: play pattern → get input → check → add tone → repeat. That's a loop!
questions.prompt: Each round adds a new random tone to the sequence. What coding concept repeats this process?

options.text: A loop -- it keeps going round after round
A display block -- it shows text
A variable -- it stores a number
Nothing -- each round is coded separately

questions.hint: Events are how programs notice things happening -- like a button being pressed!
questions.prompt: How does the program know which button the player pressed?

options.text: An event detects which button was pressed
The player tells the computer with their voice

left.text: Checking if the answer is correct

activities.prompt: Match each Simon Says game feature to the coding concept it uses!

title: My Invention
description: Design your very own program from scratch! Combine variables, loops, events, and display to create something amazing.
story_text: THIS IS IT! The big finale! Chip is SO proud of everything you've learned. You know about variables, loops, conditionals, events, display, debugging, and SO much more! Now it's time to put it ALL together and invent YOUR OWN program! Maybe it's a game, a tool, a musical instrument, or something nobody has ever thought of before. The only limit is your imagination! Let's plan your masterpiece!

questions.hint: Every great invention starts with a plan! Think about what you want to build first.
questions.prompt: What is the FIRST step when creating your own program?

options.text: Start dragging blocks randomly and hope it works
Plan what your program will do before you start coding
Ask the computer to write it for you

questions.hint: A variable is perfect for storing a number that changes -- like a score going up!
questions.prompt: You want your invention to keep track of a score. Which coding concept do you need?

options.text: A variable to store the score number
You can't track scores in code

questions.hint: The button press is an EVENT, and the sound is OUTPUT. Two concepts working together!
questions.prompt: Your invention plays a sound when Button A is pressed. Which TWO concepts are you using?

questions.hint: Real programmers debug all the time! Read each step carefully to find where things go wrong.
questions.prompt: Your program doesn't work on the first try. What should you do?

options.text: Delete everything and start over from scratch
Debug! Read the code step by step to find the mistake
Blame the device for being broken

questions.hint: The best programs are ones that do what the creator imagined! Your ideas are what make it special.
questions.prompt: What makes a program GREAT?

options.text: Using as many blocks as possible
Making it do what YOU imagined, and it's fun or useful
Copying exactly what a famous programmer made
Using only one type of block

questions.hint: This is the first step before you start building anything!
questions.template: Before coding, I should make a ___ for what my program will do.

questions.hint: It's like a labeled box that holds a value that can go up or down!
questions.template: To store information that changes, like a score, I use a ___.

questions.hint: This word means finding and fixing errors. It starts with D!
questions.template: When my code has a mistake, I need to ___ it by reading the code carefully.

questions.hint: This coding concept repeats instructions over and over like a circle!
questions.template: If I want something to happen again and again, I use a ___.

questions.hint: IF this is true, THEN do that. This is called a... it starts with C!
questions.template: To check IF something is true and then do something, I use a ___.

items.text: Think of an idea and plan what it will do
Decide which coding concepts you need (variables, loops, events, etc.)
Build the program step by step using blocks
Test the program and debug any mistakes

activities.prompt: Put the steps for creating your own invention in the correct order!
